entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 2 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  GENERATIVE AI HUB

SAP Generative AI Hub: Using the Python SDK for SAP Data Tasks

Updated March 2026: Corrected environment section — Jupyter notebooks run in SAP Business Application Studio (BAS)

Five practical SAP use cases with working Python code — across SAP AI Launchpad notebooks, local scripts, and BTP applications — using the generative-ai-hub-sdk for clean, production-ready authentication.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Once your Generative AI Hub is configured (see Part 1), the generative-ai-hub-sdk Python package handles OAuth token management automatically — removing the biggest friction point from direct API integration. This article covers SDK installation and configuration across three environments (AI Launchpad notebooks, local Python scripts, BTP applications), followed by five working code examples for real SAP data tasks: exception log summarization, HANA query analysis, ABAP/SQL code generation, master data classification, and structured data extraction from SAP documents.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Part 1 covered the complete setup path from BTP entitlements to your first API call. If you haven't configured your AI Core instance and resource group yet, start there before continuing.

This part focuses on practical Python integration — the code patterns you'll use for real SAP workloads, across the three environments where SAP practitioners most commonly run Python against the Hub.

 

Environment

Best For

Key Consideration

AI Launchpad Notebooks

Interactive development, rapid prototyping

Credentials pre-configured — fastest to start

Local Python Script

Automation, scheduled jobs, local development

Requires .env file with service key credentials

BTP Application

Production workloads, CAP integration, APIs

Credentials via BTP environment variables or secrets

SDK INSTALLATION & CONFIGURATION 

Installing and Configuring the generative-ai-hub-sdk

Why the SDK Over Direct API Calls

Part 1 showed the raw OAuth 2.0 flow using direct API calls — which is useful to understand what's happening under the hood. For any real workload, the generative-ai-hub-sdk is the better path. It handles token acquisition, token refresh, retry logic, and deployment ID resolution automatically. Your application code focuses on the task, not the authentication plumbing.

Installation

# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
pip install sap-ai-sdk-gen
 
# Optional but recommended — for HANA connection examples later
pip install hdbcli pandas python-dotenv

Configuration: AI Launchpad Notebooks

  ▶  Environment: SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) — Jupyter Notebooks 

Setting Up a BAS Dev Space for Jupyter

SAP Business Application Studio is the browser-based IDE available on BTP that supports Jupyter notebooks via the built-in Jupyter and Python extensions. This is the SAP-native interactive notebook environment for Python development — not SAP AI Launchpad, which does not provide a notebook interface.

BAS is available to all BTP customers and trial users. No additional entitlement beyond BAS subscription is required to run Jupyter notebooks.

Dev Space Setup — Step by Step

Step 1 — Create a Dev Space:

  • Open SAP Business Application Studio from your BTP subaccount
  • Click Create Dev Space
  • Name it (e.g. GenAIHub_Dev) and select SAP HANA Native Application as the type
  • Under Additional SAP Extensions, select Python Tools
  • Click Create Dev Space and wait for status to show Running
  • Click the dev space name to open it

Step 2 — Install pip and required packages:

Open a terminal in BAS (F1 → Open Terminal) and run:

# Install pip if not already available
curl https://bootstrap.pypa.io/get-pip.py > get-pip.py && python3 get-pip.py
echo "export PATH=/home/user/.local/bin:$PATH" >> ~/.bashrc && source ~/.bashrc
 
# Create a virtual environment for your project
python3 -m venv ~/.venv/genai
source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate
 
# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK and Jupyter kernel
pip install sap-ai-sdk-gen ipykernel hdbcli pandas python-dotenv
python -m ipykernel install --user --name genai --display-name 'GenAI Hub'

Step 3 — Create the AI Core credentials file:

Create .aicore-config.json in your home directory with your AI Core service key values:

{
  "AICORE_AUTH_URL": "https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": "<clientid from service key>",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": "<clientsecret from service key>",
  "AICORE_BASE_URL": "<AI_API_URL from service key>",
  "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default"
}

Step 4 — Create and run a notebook:

  • In BAS Explorer, right-click and select New File → name it test_genai.ipynb
  • Open the file — BAS will open it in the Jupyter editor
  • Click Select Kernel → Python Environments → select GenAI Hub (your venv)
  • If prompted to install ipykernel, confirm — it installs automatically

Step 5 — Verify the SDK connection in the first notebook cell:

# Verify SDK picks up credentials from .aicore-config.json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
 
client = OpenAI()
print('SDK connected successfully')

Do not commit .aicore-config.json to any Git repository. If you clone a repository into BAS and push back to Git, add .aicore-config.json to your .gitignore before the first commit. BAS has Git integration enabled by default — credential exposure via accidental commit is a real risk.

Once your dev space is running and the venv is activated, the SDK reads .aicore-config.json automatically. All five use case examples in this article run without any additional configuration changes from the BAS notebook environment.

Persisting the Virtual Environment Across BAS Sessions

BAS dev spaces suspend after inactivity. The virtual environment persists between sessions, but you must re-activate it each time you open a new terminal:

# Re-activate venv at the start of each BAS session

source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate

Tip: Add the activation command to your BAS dev space's shell profile (~/.bashrc) so it runs automatically when a new terminal opens: echo 'source ~/.venv/genai/bin/activate' >> ~/.bashrc

Configuration: Local Python Script

  ▶  Environment: Local Python Script 

For local development, create a configuration file that points the SDK to your AI Core service key credentials. Store this as .aicore-config.json in your home directory or reference it via an environment variable.

environment variable.
# ~/.aicore-config.json — from your AI Core service key
{
  "AICORE_AUTH_URL": "https://<your-subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_ID": "<clientid from service key>",
  "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET": "<clientsecret from service key>",
  "AICORE_BASE_URL": "<AI_API_URL from service key>",
  "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP": "default"
}

Never commit .aicore-config.json to version control. Add it to your .gitignore immediately. For team environments, use environment variables (AICORE_AUTH_URL, AICORE_CLIENT_ID, etc.) instead of the config file — the SDK reads both patterns automatically.

Configuration: BTP Application

  ▶  Environment: BTP Application 

In BTP applications, bind the AI Core service instance to your application. The SDK reads credentials from the VCAP_SERVICES environment variable that BTP injects automatically at runtime — no manual credential management required.

# BTP injects VCAP_SERVICES automatically when AI Core is bound
# The SDK reads it without any additional configuration
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.core.proxy_clients import get_proxy_client
 
# SDK auto-resolves from VCAP_SERVICES in BTP runtime
proxy_client = get_proxy_client('gen-ai-hub')

Once credentials are configured for your target environment, all five use case examples below work identically across all three environments. The SDK abstracts the credential source — your application code does not change.

FIVE SAP USE CASES WITH WORKING CODE 

SAP Data Tasks: Working Code Examples

The following examples cover five practical SAP use cases. Each uses the same SDK pattern — choose your preferred model by changing one parameter. All examples use Claude 3.5 Sonnet as the default; swap the model_name value for GPT-4o, Gemini, or Mistral as needed based on the model comparison in this week's LinkedIn post.

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Parse and summarize SAP SM21 / application error logs into structured root cause analysis

HANA query result analysis

Interpret query output and surface anomalies, trends, or data quality issues

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

Generate or explain code from natural language descriptions

Master data classification

Tag and categorize SAP master data records against defined classification schemes

Structured data extraction

Pull structured fields from SAP documents, change logs, or transport descriptions

Use Case 1: Summarizing SAP Exception Logs

SAP exception logs — SM21 system logs, application error dumps, job logs — are high-volume and time-consuming to triage manually. This pattern feeds raw log text to the model and returns a structured root cause summary with recommended actions.

# Use Case 1: SAP Exception Log Summarization
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
log_text = '''
  [SM21 Log Extract]
  E  RABAX  INCLUDE: ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD Line 847
  E  Runtime Error: COMPUTE_INT_PLUS_OVERFLOW
  E  Date: 2026-03-10  Time: 04:32:17
  E  Work process terminated abnormally
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis and ABAP expert.
  Analyze SAP exception logs and return:
  1. Root cause (one sentence)
  2. Affected component
  3. Severity: Critical / High / Medium / Low
  4. Recommended action
  Be concise and technical.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP log:\n{log_text}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Output example — Root cause: Integer overflow in delta load procedure at ZREPORT_DELTA_LOAD line 847 due to 32-bit counter exceeding maximum value. Component: ABAP Runtime. Severity: Critical. Action: Cast counter variable to BIGINT and redeploy procedure.

Use Case 2: Analyzing HANA Query Results

This pattern connects to HANA Cloud, executes a query, and passes the result set to the model for interpretation — surfacing anomalies, trends, or data quality issues that would otherwise require manual review.

# Use Case 2: HANA Query Result Analysis
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import pandas as pd
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443,
    user='<user>',
    password='<password>',
    encrypt=True
)
 
# Run your query and load into DataFrame
query = '''
  SELECT PLANT, MATERIAL, SUM(QUANTITY) AS TOTAL_QTY,
         COUNT(*) AS RECORD_COUNT
  FROM STOCK_OVERVIEW
  GROUP BY PLANT, MATERIAL
  ORDER BY TOTAL_QTY DESC
  LIMIT 50
'''
df = pd.read_sql(query, conn)
 
# Pass result to model for analysis
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='You are an SAP data analyst. Identify anomalies, trends, and data quality issues in SAP query results. Be specific and reference actual values from the data.'),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Analyze this SAP stock query result:\n{df.to_string()}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

Do not pass full production datasets to the model. Apply LIMIT clauses and aggregate before sending. For sensitive SAP data, review your BTP data residency settings and confirm model data handling aligns with your organization's data governance policy before deploying to production.

Use Case 3: Generating ABAP / SQL / HANA Code from Natural Language

This pattern accepts a plain-language description of a requirement and returns production-ready ABAP, SQL, or HANA SQL code. Useful for accelerating development, explaining existing code, or onboarding team members to unfamiliar syntax.

# Use Case 3: SAP Code Generation from Natural Language
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
# GPT-4o recommended for ABAP — see model comparison article
 
requirement = '''
  Write a HANA SQL procedure that:
  - Reads from a virtual table SOURCE_SCHEMA.ORDERS
  - Filters records where CHANGE_DATE > last_run_date parameter
  - Inserts new records into TARGET_SCHEMA.ORDERS_DELTA
  - Updates a control table DELTA_CONTROL with the new last_run_date
  - Handles errors with a rollback
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL developer.
  Generate clean, production-ready HANA SQL code.
  Include comments explaining key sections.
  Flag any HANA-specific considerations or limitations.'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Generate HANA SQL for this requirement:\n{requirement}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
print(response.content)

 

Use Case 4: Classifying SAP Master Data

Master data classification — tagging materials, vendors, cost centers, or GL accounts against defined schemes — is high-volume and rule-heavy. This pattern uses the model to apply a classification schema to a batch of records and return structured JSON output ready for loading back into SAP.

# Use Case 4: SAP Master Data Classification
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
materials = [
  {'MATNR': '000000000010001234', 'MAKTX': 'Hydraulic Pump Assembly 250bar'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010005678', 'MAKTX': 'Office Chair Ergonomic Mesh Black'},
  {'MATNR': '000000000010009012', 'MAKTX': 'SAP S4HANA License Annual Renewal'},
]
 
classification_schema = ['Mechanical Components', 'Office Supplies',
                         'Software & Licenses', 'Raw Materials', 'Services']
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify SAP material master records.
  Return ONLY valid JSON array. No explanation.
  Schema: {classification_schema}
  Format: [{{'MATNR': '...', 'CATEGORY': '...', 'CONFIDENCE': 'High/Medium/Low'}}]'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Classify these materials:\n{json.dumps(materials)}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
classified = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(classified, indent=2))

Prompt the model to return only JSON with no preamble or explanation when you need structured output for downstream processing. Claude is particularly consistent at following this instruction on classification tasks.

Use Case 5: Extracting Structured Data from SAP Documents

SAP landscapes accumulate semi-structured text in transport request descriptions, change log comments, functional spec attachments, and ticket notes. This pattern extracts structured fields from unstructured SAP document text — turning narrative descriptions into queryable data.

# Use Case 5: Structured Data Extraction from SAP Documents
import json
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
transport_description = '''
  TR: DEVK912345 - FI Delta Load Fix
  Developer: firstname.lastname@company.com
  Changed ZFI_DELTA_LOAD procedure to fix integer overflow on
  BKPF document counter. Root cause was 32-bit INT hitting max
  value after 2.1B records. Cast to BIGINT. Tested on DEV and QA.
  Affected tables: BKPF, BSEG. No customizing changes.
  Approved by: firstname1.lastname1@company.com on 2026-03-08
'''
 
messages = [
  SystemMessage(content='''Extract structured data from SAP transport descriptions.
  Return ONLY valid JSON. No explanation.
  Fields: transport_id, title, developer, approver, approval_date,
  affected_tables (list), root_cause, change_type, tested_systems (list)'''),
  HumanMessage(content=f'Extract fields from:\n{transport_description}')
]
 
response = llm(messages)
extracted = json.loads(response.content)
print(json.dumps(extracted, indent=2))

Choosing the Right Model per Use Case

Based on practical testing across these five patterns — and detailed in this week's LinkedIn model comparison post — here are the recommended model defaults:

 

Use Case

What the Model Does

Exception log summarization

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — consistent on long, noisy log text

HANA query result analysis

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — structured output quality, handles wide DataFrames

ABAP / SQL / HANA code generation

GPT-4o — broader SAP code knowledge, stronger on ABAP specifics

Master data classification

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — reliable JSON-only output, consistent confidence scoring

Structured data extraction

Claude 3.5 Sonnet — long-form document coherence, clean JSON output

Swap model_name='gpt-4o' or model_name='mistral-large' in any example to compare outputs. The SDK pattern is identical across all models.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 3: Building Your First Prompt Pipeline — chaining prompts, managing context, structured outputs for multi-step SAP workflows
  • Part 4: Connecting to HANA Cloud Vector Store — embedding generation, REAL_VECTOR storage, RAG on your SAP data
  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI in your SAP extension layer
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by Generative AI Hub

If you work through any of these examples and run into environment-specific issues — particularly around BTP binding or AI Launchpad notebook credential resolution — drop a comment below. The configuration edge cases are where community knowledge adds the most value.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Building Prompt Pipelines for SAP Workflows (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 3 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  GENERATIVE AI HUB

SAP Generative AI Hub: Building Prompt Pipelines for SAP Workflows

Four multi-step prompt pipelines for real SAP workloads — exception log triage, HANA code review, document processing, and master data enrichment — using sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning with the generative-ai-hub-sdk and LangChain.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: A single prompt call handles simple tasks. Complex SAP workloads — triage, code review, document processing, data enrichment — need multi-step pipelines where each prompt builds on the output of the previous one. This article covers two core pipeline patterns (sequential chaining and chain-of-thought reasoning) and four working SAP pipeline implementations using a mix of direct SDK calls and LangChain, with the generative-ai-hub-sdk handling authentication throughout.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Part 1 covered setup and configuration. Part 2 covered the Python SDK with five single-call SAP use cases. This part moves from single calls to pipelines — chaining prompts together so the output of one step becomes the input of the next.

The distinction matters in practice. A single prompt call works well for isolated tasks — summarize this log, classify this record. But real SAP workflows are multi-step: assess severity first, then summarize based on severity, then recommend action based on both. That requires a pipeline.

THE TWO CORE PIPELINE PATTERNS 

Pattern 1: Sequential Prompt Chaining

Sequential chaining passes the output of one prompt directly as input to the next. Each step is focused on a single task, and the chain builds context progressively. This produces more reliable results than asking one prompt to do everything — the model performs better when each step has a narrow, well-defined responsibility.

Step 1: Classify → Step 2: Analyze → Step 3: Recommend

The key design principle: each step should do one thing well. Resist the temptation to combine steps. A prompt that classifies AND summarizes AND recommends produces lower quality output than three focused prompts in sequence.

Pattern 2: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) Reasoning

Chain-of-thought prompting instructs the model to reason through a problem explicitly before producing a final answer. For SAP workloads where the output needs to be traceable and justifiable — risk assessments, code review findings, data quality decisions — CoT produces outputs that practitioners can audit and defend.

Input context →	Reasoning steps (visible) → Conclusion → Action

CoT is particularly valuable in enterprise SAP contexts because it makes the model's reasoning transparent. When a pipeline flags a transport as high-risk or recommends a data fix, stakeholders can see exactly why — not just what the conclusion was.

CoT increases token consumption and response time compared to direct answer prompts. Use it for high-stakes decisions (risk assessment, code review, data quality) where traceability justifies the cost. For high-volume classification or extraction tasks, direct prompts from Part 2 are more appropriate.

PIPELINE 1 OF 4 

  ⬡  SAP Exception Log Triage: Classify → Summarize → Recommend 

Exception Log Triage Pipeline

This pipeline takes raw SAP exception log text and runs it through three focused steps: first classify severity, then produce a structured summary calibrated to that severity level, then generate recommended actions appropriate for the severity. Splitting these into three steps produces materially better output than asking one prompt to do all three.

Raw log input →	Classify severity → Structured summary → Recommended actions

 

# Pipeline 1: SAP Exception Log Triage
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
# Step 1: Classify severity
step1_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    Classify this SAP exception log severity as one of:
    CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
    Return ONLY the severity label. Nothing else.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Classify severity:\n{log_input}')
]
severity = llm(step1_messages).content.strip()
# Step 2: Structured summary — informed by severity from Step 1
step2_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content=f'''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    This is a {severity} severity SAP exception.
    Provide a structured summary with:
    - Root cause (one sentence)
    - Affected component
    - Business impact
    Be concise. Severity context: {severity}.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Summarize this log:\n{log_input}')
]
summary = llm(step2_messages).content.strip()
# Step 3: Recommended actions — uses both severity and summary
step3_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP Basis expert.
    Based on the severity and summary provided, give:
    1. Immediate action (within 1 hour)
    2. Root cause fix
    3. Prevention measure
    Be specific to SAP. No generic advice.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Severity: {severity}\nSummary: {summary}\nLog: {log_input}')
]
actions = llm(step3_messages).content.strip()
# Final structured output
print(f'SEVERITY: {severity}')
print(f'\nSUMMARY:\n{summary}')
print(f'\nRECOMMENDED ACTIONS:\n{actions}')

Each step passes its output forward as context. Step 2 knows the severity before writing the summary. Step 3 knows both severity and summary before recommending actions. This progressive context building is what makes pipeline output significantly more useful than a single-prompt approach.

  PIPELINE 2 OF 4 

  ⬡  HANA Code Review: Analyze → Explain → Suggest Fix 

HANA Code Review Pipeline

This pipeline applies chain-of-thought reasoning to HANA SQL code review. Rather than asking the model to jump directly to recommendations, CoT forces it to reason through the code structure first — producing more accurate findings and traceable justifications that developers can act on with confidence.

HANA code input → CoT: reason through code → Structured findings → Suggested fixes

 

# Pipeline 2: HANA Code Review with Chain-of-Thought
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
# GPT-4o for HANA code — strongest on SAP SQL specifics
 
hana_code = '''
  CREATE PROCEDURE LOAD_DELTA_ORDERS
  AS BEGIN
    DECLARE lv_count INT;
    SELECT COUNT(*) INTO lv_count FROM SRC.ORDERS
    WHERE CHANGE_DATE > (SELECT MAX(LOAD_DATE) FROM CTRL_TABLE);
    INSERT INTO TGT.ORDERS_DELTA
    SELECT * FROM SRC.ORDERS
    WHERE CHANGE_DATE > (SELECT MAX(LOAD_DATE) FROM CTRL_TABLE);
    UPDATE CTRL_TABLE SET LOAD_DATE = CURRENT_DATE;
  END;
'''
# Step 1: CoT reasoning — model thinks before it concludes
step1_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL architect.
    Think through this HANA procedure step by step before forming conclusions.
    Reason about:
    - Data type risks and overflow potential
    - Performance implications (full scans, missing indexes)
    - Transaction safety and rollback handling
    - Subquery execution patterns
    Show your reasoning explicitly before any findings.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Review this HANA procedure:\n{hana_code}')
]
cot_reasoning = llm(step1_messages).content.strip()
# Step 2: Structured findings — informed by CoT reasoning
step2_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''Based on your reasoning, produce structured findings.
    Format each finding as:
    ISSUE: [description]
    SEVERITY: Critical / High / Medium / Low
    LINE: [line number or section]
    No additional commentary.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Reasoning:\n{cot_reasoning}\n\nCode:\n{hana_code}')
]
findings = llm(step2_messages).content.strip()
# Step 3: Suggested fixes for each finding
step3_messages = [
    SystemMessage(content='''You are a senior SAP HANA SQL developer.
    For each finding, provide the corrected HANA SQL code.
    Show original line and corrected replacement.
    Explain the fix in one sentence per issue.'''),
    HumanMessage(content=f'Findings:\n{findings}\n\nOriginal code:\n{hana_code}')
]
fixes = llm(step3_messages).content.strip()
 
print(f'REASONING:\n{cot_reasoning}')
print(f'\nFINDINGS:\n{findings}')
print(f'\nSUGGESTED FIXES:\n{fixes}')

The CoT reasoning step is the key differentiator here. Without it, the model jumps to conclusions. With it, the findings section is grounded in explicit reasoning that a developer or architect can verify — which is critical for code review outputs that will be acted on in production systems.

PIPELINE 3 OF 4 

  ⬡  SAP Document Processing: Parse → Extract → Structure 

Document Processing Pipeline

SAP landscapes accumulate large volumes of semi-structured documents — functional specs, migration runbooks, transport logs, configuration workbooks. This pipeline uses LangChain's prompt templates to process documents consistently at scale: parse the document type and structure, extract key fields, then output clean structured JSON ready for downstream use.

Document input → Parse type & structure → Extract fields → Structured JSON output

 

# Pipeline 3: SAP Document Processing with LangChain Templates
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
parser = StrOutputParser()
# Step 1: Parse document type and identify structure
parse_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are an SAP document analyst.
    Identify the document type and its key sections.
    Return ONLY: document_type, sections (list), primary_subject'''),
    ('human', 'Analyze this SAP document:\n{document}')
])
# Step 2: Extract fields based on document type
extract_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are an SAP document analyst.
    Document type: {doc_type}
    Extract all relevant fields for this document type.
    Return ONLY valid JSON. No explanation or preamble.'''),
    ('human', 'Extract fields from:\n{document}')
])
# Step 3: Validate and structure the extracted JSON
structure_prompt = ChatPromptTemplate.from_messages([
    ('system', '''You are a data quality validator.
    Review the extracted fields for completeness and consistency.
    Add a confidence score (0.0-1.0) and flag any missing critical fields.
    Return the final structured JSON with confidence and flags added.'''),
    ('human', 'Validate and structure:\n{extracted}')
])
# Build LangChain LCEL pipeline using pipe operator
parse_chain = parse_prompt | llm | parser
extract_chain = extract_prompt | llm | parser
structure_chain = structure_prompt | llm | parser
# Run pipeline sequentially
sap_document = '''
  Functional Spec: S4H Migration - Customer Master
  Author: jane.doe@company.com  Date: 2026-03-01
  Scope: Migrate 450,000 customer master records from ECC 6.0
  Source: KUNNR, KNA1, KNVV tables
  Target: S/4HANA BP (Business Partner) model
  Key rules: Deduplicate by tax ID, map account groups,
  preserve credit limits, validate IBAN format
  Sign-off required: Data Owner, Finance Lead
'''
 
doc_structure = parse_chain.invoke({'document': sap_document})
doc_type = 'Functional Specification'
 
extracted = extract_chain.invoke({
    'document': sap_document,
    'doc_type': doc_type
})
 
final_output = structure_chain.invoke({'extracted': extracted})
print(final_output)

LangChain's pipe operator (|) makes the chain readable and easy to modify — swap a prompt template or model at any step without touching the rest of the pipeline. The LCEL pattern also supports async execution and streaming when needed for production workloads.

 PIPELINE 4 OF 4 

  ⬡  Master Data Enrichment: Extract → Validate → Classify 

Master Data Enrichment Pipeline

Master data in SAP is rarely clean enough for direct AI consumption. This pipeline combines sequential chaining with CoT validation to enrich raw master data records: extract available attributes, apply CoT reasoning to validate completeness and consistency, then classify against a defined taxonomy. The CoT validation step is the difference between a classification pipeline and a trustworthy one.

Raw master data → Extract attributes → CoT: validate quality → Classify + confidence

 

# Pipeline 4: Master Data Enrichment with CoT Validation
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
raw_records = [
    {'MATNR': '1000234', 'MAKTX': 'Pump assy hydraulic 250bar SAE',
     'MTART': 'ROH', 'MATKL': '', 'MEINS': 'EA', 'BRGEW': '12.5'},
    {'MATNR': '1000891', 'MAKTX': 'CHAIR OFFICE MESH BLK ERGONOMIC',
     'MTART': 'HIBE', 'MATKL': 'OFFICE', 'MEINS': 'EA', 'BRGEW': ''},
]
 
taxonomy = {
    'Mechanical Components': 'Industrial parts, assemblies, hydraulic/pneumatic',
    'Office Supplies': 'Furniture, stationery, office equipment',
    'Raw Materials': 'Unprocessed inputs for manufacturing',
    'MRO': 'Maintenance, repair and operations items',
}
 
enriched_records = []
 
for record in raw_records:
    # Step 1: Extract and normalize attributes
    step1 = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP master data expert.
        Extract and normalize attributes from this SAP material record.
        Infer missing fields where possible from the description.
        Return ONLY JSON with fields: material_number, description_clean,
        material_type, unit, weight_kg, inferred_fields (list).'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Extract from: {json.dumps(record)}')
    ]
    extracted = llm(step1).content.strip()
    # Step 2: CoT validation — reason through data quality
    step2 = [
        SystemMessage(content='''You are a data quality expert.
        Reason through this master data record step by step:
        1. Are required fields present and consistent?
        2. Does the description match the material type?
        3. Are there contradictions or suspicious values?
        4. What is your overall quality confidence (High/Medium/Low)?
        Show reasoning, then conclude with: QUALITY: [level] and ISSUES: [list]'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Validate this record:\n{extracted}')
    ]
    validation = llm(step2).content.strip()
    # Step 3: Classify against taxonomy using extracted + validated data
    step3 = [
        SystemMessage(content=f'''Classify this SAP material into exactly one category.
        Taxonomy: {json.dumps(taxonomy)}
        Use the validation findings to inform confidence.
        Return ONLY JSON: category, confidence (High/Medium/Low), reason (one sentence)'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Extracted: {extracted}\nValidation: {validation}')
    ]
    classification = json.loads(llm(step3).content.strip())
 
    enriched_records.append({
        'material': record['MATNR'],
        'extracted': json.loads(extracted),
        'validation': validation,
        'classification': classification
    })
 
for r in enriched_records:
    print(f"Material {r['material']}: {r['classification']['category']} ",
          f"({r['classification']['confidence']}) — {r['classification']['reason']}")

The CoT validation step will occasionally surface data issues you did not expect — missing material groups, inconsistent units of measure, description/type mismatches. This is the pipeline working correctly. Treat validation findings as data quality signals worth feeding back to your SAP master data governance process.

 PIPELINE DESIGN PRINCIPLES 

What Makes SAP Prompt Pipelines Reliable

Across these four pipelines, a consistent set of design decisions separate reliable production output from inconsistent prototype output:

 

Design Principle

Why It Matters

One responsibility per step

Each prompt does one thing. Classify only. Summarize only. Recommend only. Combined prompts produce lower quality output across all tasks.

Pass context forward explicitly

Every downstream step receives the outputs of upstream steps as explicit context. Never assume the model remembers previous calls — it doesn't.

Use CoT for high-stakes decisions

When output needs to be auditable — risk assessments, code review, data quality — add a reasoning step before the conclusion. It costs more tokens but the output is traceable.

Enforce JSON for structured output

Instruct the model to return only JSON with no preamble when downstream code will parse the response. Claude and GPT-4o both follow this reliably when the instruction is explicit.

Choose model per step, not per pipeline

A pipeline can use different models at different steps. Use GPT-4o for HANA code steps, Claude for summarization and structured output steps. The SDK makes this a one-line change per step.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 4: Connecting to HANA Cloud Vector Store — embedding generation, REAL_VECTOR storage, RAG on your SAP data with the Generative AI Hub
  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI pipelines in your SAP extension layer
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core pipelines

If you build any of these pipelines and find that a particular step produces inconsistent output, the fix is almost always in the system prompt — either narrowing the scope of the step or making the output format instruction more explicit. Drop a comment below with the pattern and I'll share what worked in our implementation.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store (Part 4 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 4 OF 6  |  SAP AI CORE  |  HANA CLOUD  |  RAG

SAP Generative AI Hub: RAG on SAP Data with HANA Cloud Vector Store

End-to-end RAG pipeline on SAP data — REAL_VECTOR schema setup, SAP embedding model, document chunking, similarity search, and four working use cases: knowledge base search, data dictionary retrieval, transport log search, and master data context retrieval.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 4 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: HANA Cloud's native REAL_VECTOR data type turns your existing SAP database into a vector store — no separate infrastructure required. Combined with the SAP embedding model through AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, you get a fully BTP-native RAG pipeline on your SAP data. This article covers the complete path: schema setup, embedding generation, chunking strategy, similarity search, and four SAP-specific RAG use cases with working code.

Series Context

This is Part 4 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–3 covered setup, Python SDK integration, and prompt pipelines. This part connects those pipelines to persistent SAP data through retrieval-augmented generation — the pattern that makes foundation model outputs grounded in your actual SAP context rather than general training data.

RAG is the architectural bridge between the prompt pipelines from Part 3 and the production SAP applications coming in Parts 5 and 6. Understanding it here makes the CAP and Joule integration articles that follow significantly easier to follow.

WHY RAG FOR SAP WORKLOADS 

Why Foundation Models Need RAG in SAP Contexts

Foundation models — even the most capable ones — have no knowledge of your SAP landscape. They do not know your custom Z-tables, your transport history, your material master conventions, your configured process variants, or the accumulated institutional knowledge in your SAP documentation. When you ask a foundation model an SAP question without context, it answers from general training data. That is not good enough for enterprise use.

RAG solves this by retrieving relevant context from your own SAP data at query time and passing it to the model alongside the question. The model's answer is grounded in your actual system — your data, your configuration, your history.

HANA Cloud makes this particularly clean for SAP practitioners because the vector store lives in the same database as your SAP data. There is no separate vector database to provision, secure, or maintain. Your embeddings sit alongside your operational data in HANA, with the same access controls, backup policies, and compliance posture you already manage.

SAP data sources → Chunk + embed → Store in HANA REAL_VECTOR → Query similarity search → Grounded model response

STEP 1: HANA VECTOR STORE SCHEMA SETUP 

Creating the REAL_VECTOR Schema in HANA Cloud

What REAL_VECTOR Is

REAL_VECTOR is a native HANA Cloud data type introduced for vector storage. It stores fixed-dimension floating-point arrays — the numerical representations (embeddings) that capture the semantic meaning of text. HANA's vector engine supports cosine similarity and L2 distance calculations directly in SQL, which means your similarity search runs inside the database without moving data out.

REAL_VECTOR is a HANA Cloud feature — it is not available in on-premise SAP HANA. Confirm your HANA Cloud instance version supports vector engine capabilities before proceeding. As of 2026, vector store functionality requires HANA Cloud QRC 1/2024 or later.

Schema Creation

Create a dedicated schema for your vector store tables. Keeping vector store tables separate from operational SAP data makes access control and maintenance significantly cleaner.

-- Create dedicated schema for vector store
CREATE SCHEMA AI_VECTOR_STORE;
 
-- General purpose document embedding table
CREATE TABLE AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (
    EMBEDDING_ID    NVARCHAR(100)   NOT NULL PRIMARY KEY,
    SOURCE_TYPE     NVARCHAR(50)    NOT NULL,  -- e.g. 'KB', 'DDIC', 'TRANSPORT'
    SOURCE_ID       NVARCHAR(200)   NOT NULL,  -- original record identifier
    CHUNK_INDEX     INTEGER         NOT NULL,  -- chunk position within source doc
    CHUNK_TEXT      NCLOB           NOT NULL,  -- original text of this chunk
    EMBEDDING       REAL_VECTOR(1536),         -- embedding vector (1536 dims)
    METADATA        NCLOB,                     -- JSON metadata for filtering
    CREATED_AT      TIMESTAMP       DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);
 
-- Index for fast source type filtering before vector search
CREATE INDEX IDX_DOC_EMB_SOURCE ON AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS (SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID);

The EMBEDDING column dimension (1536) must match the output dimension of your embedding model exactly. The SAP embedding model used in this series outputs 1536-dimensional vectors. If you change embedding models, you must recreate the table with the correct dimension — existing embeddings cannot be reused with a different model.

STEP 2: GENERATING EMBEDDINGS VIA AI CORE 

Generating Embeddings with the SAP Embedding Model

Why the SAP Embedding Model

The SAP embedding model available through AI Core's Generative AI Hub is tuned for enterprise and business content — the kind of domain-specific language that appears in SAP documentation, functional specs, transport descriptions, and master data. For SAP-specific RAG applications, it consistently outperforms general-purpose embedding models on retrieval precision for SAP terminology and process language.

Using it through the Hub also keeps the entire pipeline within the BTP trust boundary — the same governance, audit logging, and data residency controls that apply to your generative AI calls apply to embedding generation too.

Document Chunking Strategy

Chunking — splitting source documents into segments before embedding — is one of the most consequential decisions in a RAG pipeline. Chunks that are too large dilute the embedding signal. Chunks that are too small lose context. For SAP content, the following strategy produced the best retrieval results across our four use cases:

 

Source Type

Chunk Size

Chunking Approach

SAP Knowledge Base articles

500–700 tokens per chunk

Paragraph boundaries — keep procedural steps together

HANA data dictionary descriptions

Full field/table description as single chunk

Short content — no chunking needed

Transport / change log entries

One transport record per chunk

Natural boundary — each transport is self-contained

Master data descriptions

One record per chunk with key fields as prefix

Prefix with MATNR/LIFNR for retrieval context

Embedding Generation Code

# Step 2: Generate embeddings using SAP embedding model via AI Core
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.openai import OpenAI
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json, uuid
 
# Initialize SDK client — credentials from environment or config file
client = OpenAI()
 
# Connect to HANA Cloud
conn = hdb.connect(
    address='<your-hana-host>.hanacloud.ondemand.com',
    port=443, user='<user>', password='<password>', encrypt=True
)
 
def generate_embedding(text: str) -> list:
    """Generate embedding vector using SAP embedding model via AI Core."""
    response = client.embeddings.create(
        model='text-embedding-ada-002',
        # SAP-provided embedding model — routed through AI Core
        input=text
    )
    return response.data[0].embedding
 
def chunk_text(text: str, chunk_size: int = 600, overlap: int = 50) -> list:
    """Split text into overlapping chunks by word count."""
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    for i in range(0, len(words), chunk_size - overlap):
        chunk = ' '.join(words[i:i + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks
 
def store_embeddings(source_type: str, source_id: str,
                     text: str, metadata: dict = None):
    """Chunk, embed, and store a document in HANA vector store."""
    chunks = chunk_text(text)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    for idx, chunk in enumerate(chunks):
        embedding = generate_embedding(chunk)
        embedding_id = str(uuid.uuid4())
 
        cursor.execute('''
            INSERT INTO AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            (EMBEDDING_ID, SOURCE_TYPE, SOURCE_ID, CHUNK_INDEX,
             CHUNK_TEXT, EMBEDDING, METADATA)
            VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?), ?)
        ''', (
            embedding_id, source_type, source_id, idx,
            chunk, str(embedding),
            json.dumps(metadata) if metadata else None
        ))
 
    conn.commit()
    print(f'Stored {len(chunks)} chunks for {source_type}/{source_id}')

Embedding generation has a cost per token through AI Core. For initial ingestion of large SAP document sets, run embedding generation in batches with rate limiting and store results immediately — do not regenerate embeddings on every pipeline run. Re-embed only when source documents change.

STEP 3: SIMILARITY SEARCH IN HANA SQL 

Querying the Vector Store with COSINE_SIMILARITY

How HANA Vector Search Works

HANA Cloud's vector engine computes similarity between a query embedding and all stored embeddings directly in SQL using built-in functions. COSINE_SIMILARITY measures the angle between vectors — returning values between -1 and 1, where 1 is identical. For SAP document retrieval, cosine similarity consistently outperforms L2 distance because it is scale-invariant — document length differences do not artificially inflate distance scores.

Similarity Search Function

# Step 3: Similarity search — retrieve top-k most relevant chunks
def similarity_search(query: str, source_type: str = None,
                      top_k: int = 5) -> list:
    """
    Embed query and retrieve most similar chunks from HANA vector store.
    Optionally filter by source_type for targeted retrieval.
    """
    query_embedding = generate_embedding(query)
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Build query with optional source type filter
    source_filter = "AND SOURCE_TYPE = ?" if source_type else ""
    params = [str(query_embedding), top_k]
    if source_type:
        params.insert(1, source_type)
 
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT TOP ?
            SOURCE_TYPE,
            SOURCE_ID,
            CHUNK_INDEX,
            CHUNK_TEXT,
            METADATA,
            COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?)) AS SIMILARITY_SCORE
        FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
        WHERE 1=1 {source_filter}
        ORDER BY SIMILARITY_SCORE DESC
    ''', params)
 
    results = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        results.append({
            'source_type': row[0],
            'source_id':   row[1],
            'chunk_index': row[2],
            'chunk_text':  row[3],
            'metadata':    json.loads(row[4]) if row[4] else {},
            'score':       float(row[5])
        })
    return results

Filter by SOURCE_TYPE before running vector similarity whenever you know which data domain is relevant to the query. Narrowing the search space improves both retrieval precision and query performance — especially as your vector store grows across multiple SAP data domains.

STEP 4: THE COMPLETE RAG PIPELINE 

Assembling the End-to-End RAG Pipeline

With embedding generation and similarity search in place, the RAG pipeline itself is straightforward: embed the query, retrieve relevant chunks, assemble them as context, and pass context plus query to the foundation model. The model's response is grounded in your retrieved SAP data.

# Step 4: Complete RAG pipeline function
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='claude-3.5-sonnet')
 
def rag_query(question: str, source_type: str = None,
              top_k: int = 5, system_context: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Full RAG pipeline: retrieve relevant SAP context,
    then generate grounded response using foundation model.
    """
    # Step 1: Retrieve relevant context from HANA vector store
    retrieved = similarity_search(question, source_type, top_k)
 
    # Step 2: Assemble context from retrieved chunks
    context_blocks = []
    for r in retrieved:
        context_blocks.append(
            f"[Source: {r['source_type']} / {r['source_id']} ",
            f"| Score: {r['score']:.3f}]\n{r['chunk_text']}"
        )
    context = '\n\n---\n\n'.join(context_blocks)
 
    # Step 3: Build grounded prompt with retrieved context
    default_system = '''You are an SAP expert assistant.
    Answer questions using ONLY the context provided below.
    If the context does not contain enough information to answer,
    say so explicitly — do not use general knowledge.
    Always reference the source when citing specific information.'''
 
    messages = [
        SystemMessage(content=system_context or default_system),
        HumanMessage(content=f'''
CONTEXT FROM SAP DATA:\n{context}
\n\nQUESTION: {question}''')
    ]
 
    # Step 4: Generate grounded response
    return llm(messages).content

The instruction 'Answer using ONLY the context provided — do not use general knowledge' is critical for SAP RAG pipelines. Without it, the model supplements retrieved context with training data, which can introduce information about standard SAP that contradicts your specific configuration or customizations.

FOUR SAP RAG USE CASES 

Use Case Examples

  ▶  Use Case 1: SAP Knowledge Base Search 

Grounded Q&A on SAP Documentation

Ingest your SAP knowledge base articles, configuration guides, and internal runbooks into the vector store. The RAG pipeline then answers practitioner questions grounded in your actual documented procedures — not generic SAP guidance from the model's training data.

# Ingest SAP knowledge base articles
kb_articles = [
    {
        'id': 'KB-2024-001',
        'title': 'HANA Delta Load: Handling Large Volume Tables',
        'content': '''When delta loading tables exceeding 500M records...
        Use hash-based partitioning with MOD(ABS(TO_BIGINT(KEY_FIELD)), N)
        to distribute load across parallel workers. Monitor memory via
        M_HEAP_MEMORY view. Control table must track per-partition watermarks.'''
    },
]
 
for article in kb_articles:
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='KB',
        source_id=article['id'],
        text=f"{article['title']}\n\n{article['content']}",
        metadata={'title': article['title']}
    )
 
# Query the knowledge base
answer = rag_query(
    question='How should I handle integer overflow in HANA delta loads?',
    source_type='KB',
    top_k=3
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 2: HANA Data Dictionary Search 

Natural Language Queries on Table and Field Metadata

Embed HANA data dictionary metadata — table descriptions, field definitions, domain values, foreign key relationships — so practitioners can find relevant tables and fields using natural language instead of needing to know exact technical names upfront.

# Ingest HANA data dictionary metadata from HANA system views
cursor = conn.cursor()
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS,
           STRING_AGG(C.COLUMN_NAME || ': ' || IFNULL(C.COMMENTS,''),  ', ')
           AS FIELD_LIST
    FROM SYS.TABLES T
    JOIN SYS.TABLE_COLUMNS C ON T.TABLE_NAME = C.TABLE_NAME
    WHERE T.SCHEMA_NAME = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND T.TABLE_NAME LIKE 'Z%'
    -- Scope to your custom Z-tables first
    GROUP BY T.TABLE_NAME, T.COMMENTS
    LIMIT 500
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    table_name, description, fields = row
    content = f'Table: {table_name}\nDescription: {description}\nFields: {fields}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='DDIC',
        source_id=table_name,
        text=content,
        metadata={'table_name': table_name}
    )
 
# Find relevant tables using natural language
answer = rag_query(
    question='Which tables store customer payment terms and credit limits?',
    source_type='DDIC',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 3: Transport and Change Log Retrieval 

Semantic Search Across SAP Change History

Transport request descriptions and change logs are high-value institutional knowledge that is almost always searched by keyword — missing semantic connections between related changes. Embedding transport history enables semantic retrieval: find transports related to a concept or problem even when the exact terms don't match.

# Ingest transport descriptions from E070 / E07T
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER,
           STRING_AGG(O.OBJECT || '/' || O.OBJ_NAME, ', ') AS OBJECTS
    FROM E070 T
    JOIN E071 O ON T.TRKORR = O.TRKORR
    WHERE T.AS4DATE >= '20250101'
    AND T.TRSTATUS = 'R'
    -- Released transports only
    GROUP BY T.TRKORR, T.AS4TEXT, T.AS4DATE, T.AS4USER
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    tr_id, description, date, user, objects = row
    content = f'''Transport: {tr_id}
Description: {description}
Changed by: {user} on {date}
Objects: {objects}'''
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='TRANSPORT',
        source_id=tr_id,
        text=content,
        metadata={'tr_id': tr_id, 'date': str(date), 'user': user}
    )
 
# Semantic search across transport history
answer = rag_query(
    question='What changes were made to the FI posting handler in 2025?',
    source_type='TRANSPORT',
    top_k=5
)
print(answer)

  ▶  Use Case 4: Master Data Context Retrieval for AI Classification 

Retrieval-Augmented Master Data Classification

This use case combines the master data classification pipeline from Part 3 with RAG — retrieving similar previously-classified records as few-shot examples at query time. The model classifies new records guided by real examples from your own SAP master data, not generic training examples. This produces significantly higher accuracy on company-specific classification schemes.

# Ingest already-classified master data records as reference examples
cursor.execute('''
    SELECT MATNR, MAKTX, MATKL, MTART, ZZCATEGORY
    FROM MARA JOIN MAKT ON MARA.MATNR = MAKT.MATNR
    WHERE MAKT.SPRAS = 'E'
    AND ZZCATEGORY IS NOT NULL
    -- Only records with confirmed classifications
    LIMIT 1000
''')
 
for row in cursor.fetchall():
    matnr, desc, matkl, mtart, category = row
    content = f'Material: {matnr} | {desc} | Type: {mtart} | Category: {category}'
    store_embeddings(
        source_type='MASTER_DATA',
        source_id=matnr,
        text=content,
        metadata={'matnr': matnr, 'category': category, 'mtart': mtart}
    )
 
# Classify new material using retrieved similar records as examples
new_material = 'Hydraulic seal kit 250bar SAP-HYD-250'
 
answer = rag_query(
    question=f'Classify this new material: {new_material}',
    source_type='MASTER_DATA',
    top_k=5,
    system_context='''You are an SAP master data classifier.
    Use the similar records provided as classification examples.
    Return JSON only: {"category": "...", "confidence": "High/Medium/Low",
    "based_on": [list of similar MATNR values used as reference]}'''
)
print(answer)

Retrieval-augmented classification using your own historical master data consistently outperforms zero-shot classification because the model is guided by your specific category labels and conventions — not generic industry taxonomies. The more classified records you ingest, the more accurate the retrieval and the better the classification output.

DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going to Production

 

Consideration

Guidance

Embedding consistency

Never mix embedding models across records in the same table. A similarity search comparing vectors from different models produces meaningless scores. Pick one model and stick to it — if you change models, re-embed everything.

Chunk overlap

50-token overlap between chunks prevents relevant context from being split across chunk boundaries. Adjust overlap up for dense technical content like ABAP code or configuration tables.

Score thresholds

Not all retrieved chunks are relevant. Filter results below a COSINE_SIMILARITY score of 0.75 before passing to the model. Low-score chunks add noise and reduce answer quality.

Re-embedding cadence

SAP data changes. Transport descriptions, knowledge base articles, and master data classifications go stale. Build a delta re-embedding job that runs on the same schedule as your delta data loads.

Source type filtering

Always filter by SOURCE_TYPE when the query domain is known. Mixing transport history chunks with knowledge base chunks in a single retrieval pass produces lower precision than targeted retrieval from the right domain.

What's Next in This Series

  • Part 5: Integrating AI Core into a BTP CAP Application — production-grade AI with OData APIs, S/4HANA integration, and the RAG pipeline from this article embedded in a CAP service
  • Part 6: Extending SAP Joule with Foundation Models — custom Joule skills backed by AI Core, with HANA vector store retrieval for grounded natural language interaction across SAP applications

The vector store you build in this article carries forward into Parts 5 and 6. If you're following the series end-to-end, the RAG pipeline here is the foundation that makes the CAP application and Joule extension in the next two parts genuinely useful rather than demo-grade. Drop a comment if you hit issues with REAL_VECTOR column dimensions or similarity score thresholds — these are the two most common friction points in production deployments.

entry.title: SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP (Part 5 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES: PART 5 OF 6  |  SAP BTP  |  CAP  |  AI CORE

SAP Generative AI Hub: Building AI-Powered CAP Applications on BTP

End-to-end architecture for AI-powered SAP extension applications using CAP — project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, Generative AI Hub integration in service handlers, SAP Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Part 5 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is the standard framework for building SAP extension applications on BTP. When combined with AI Core and the Generative AI Hub, CAP becomes the production layer that exposes your AI pipelines as governed OData and REST APIs — consumable by S/4HANA, Fiori, Integration Suite, and any external system. This article covers the complete path: CAP project setup, AI Core service binding, HANA Cloud persistence, calling the Generative AI Hub from CAP service handlers, Integration Suite connectivity, and Cloud Foundry deployment — built around two production-ready use cases.

Series Context

This is Part 5 of a 6-part series on SAP Generative AI Hub. Parts 1–4 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, and RAG on HANA Cloud vector store. This part moves from standalone Python scripts to production SAP applications — packaging the AI capabilities from previous parts into CAP services that integrate natively with the SAP ecosystem.

The RAG pipeline from Part 4 and the prompt pipelines from Part 3 are referenced throughout this article. If you are following the series end-to-end, those implementations carry forward directly into the CAP service handlers built here.

WHY CAP FOR SAP AI APPLICATIONS 

Why CAP Is the Right Production Layer for SAP AI

SAP CAP (Cloud Application Programming Model) is SAP's opinionated framework for building cloud-native extension applications on BTP. It handles OData protocol compliance, HANA Cloud schema management, BTP service binding, multi-tenancy, and authorization out of the box — letting you focus on business logic rather than infrastructure plumbing.

For SAP AI applications specifically, CAP provides three capabilities that matter in production:

  • Native BTP service binding — AI Core, HANA Cloud, and Destination Service credentials are injected automatically at runtime via VCAP_SERVICES, eliminating manual credential management
  • OData and REST API exposure — your AI pipelines become consumable APIs that S/4HANA, SAP Fiori, and SAP Integration Suite can call directly without custom integration code
  • HANA Cloud persistence — the same HANA Cloud instance that holds your vector store from Part 4 also persists CAP entities, keeping your entire data layer consolidated

The result is that the prompt pipelines and RAG architecture from earlier in this series stop being Python scripts and become governed, auditable, production-grade SAP services — with the access controls, monitoring, and deployment lifecycle that enterprise SAP landscapes require.

CAP is not a replacement for AI Core or the Generative AI Hub — it is the application layer that sits in front of them. AI Core handles model access, authentication, and governance. CAP handles API exposure, service binding, and SAP ecosystem integration. Both are required for production SAP AI applications.

ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

End-to-End Architecture

The architecture for an AI-powered CAP application on BTP connects five layers:

S/4HANA / Fiori / Int. Suite → CAP OData/REST API → AI Core + Gen AI Hub →	HANA Cloud + Vector Store → Foundation Models

 

Layer

Role

S/4HANA / Fiori / Integration Suite

Consumers of the CAP API — send queries, receive AI-grounded responses

CAP Application (BTP CF)

OData and REST service layer — handles requests, calls AI Core, returns responses

SAP AI Core + Generative AI Hub

Foundation model access — GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini, Mistral via governed API

HANA Cloud

Dual purpose — CAP entity persistence AND vector store from Part 4

SAP Integration Suite

Orchestrates AI-augmented process flows across SAP and non-SAP systems

STEP 1: CAP PROJECT SETUP 

Setting Up the CAP Project

Prerequisites

Before initializing the CAP project, confirm the following are in place on your development machine:

  • Node.js 20 or higher — CAP requires a supported LTS version
  • @sap/cds-dk installed globally — the CAP development kit
  • Cloud Foundry CLI (cf) — for BTP deployment
  • SAP BTP subaccount with AI Core (Extended plan), HANA Cloud, and BTP CF runtime entitlements
  • AI Core resource group configured and service key available (covered in Part 1)

Initialize the CAP Project

# Install CAP development kit globally if not already installed
npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
 
# Initialize new CAP project
cds init ai-sap-service
cd ai-sap-service
 
# Install dependencies
npm install
 
# Add HANA Cloud support
cds add hana
 
# Add Cloud Foundry deployment configuration
cds add cf-manifest
 
# Install the SAP Generative AI Hub SDK
npm install @sap-ai-sdk/langchain @sap-ai-sdk/ai-api

The @sap-ai-sdk/langchain package is SAP's official JavaScript/TypeScript SDK for the Generative AI Hub — the Node.js equivalent of the generative-ai-hub-sdk used in Parts 2–4. It handles OAuth token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically within your CAP service handlers.

Project Structure

After initialization, your CAP project structure looks like this:

ai-sap-service/
├── db/
│   └── schema.cds          # HANA Cloud entity definitions
├── srv/
│   ├── ai-service.cds      # OData service definition
│   └── ai-service.js       # Service handler — AI logic lives here
├── app/                    # Optional Fiori UI
├── mta.yaml               # BTP deployment descriptor
├── package.json
└── .cdsrc.json            # CAP configuration

STEP 2: DATA MODEL AND SERVICE DEFINITION 

Defining the Data Model and OData Service

HANA Cloud Entity Schema (db/schema.cds)

Define the entities that CAP will deploy to HANA Cloud. These persist AI query history, knowledge base documents, and search results — enabling audit trails and caching for production workloads.

// db/schema.cds
namespace ai.sap.service;
 
entity AIQueryLog {
    key ID          : UUID;
    query           : String(2000);
    response        : LargeString;
    modelUsed       : String(100);
    sourceType      : String(50);   // 'MASTER_DATA' | 'KNOWLEDGE_BASE'
    confidenceScore : Decimal(4,3);
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
    createdBy       : String(100);
}
 
entity KnowledgeBaseDoc {
    key ID          : UUID;
    title           : String(500);
    content         : LargeString;
    category        : String(100);
    embeddingStatus : String(20);   // 'PENDING' | 'EMBEDDED' | 'FAILED'
    createdAt       : Timestamp;
}

OData Service Definition (srv/ai-service.cds)

Define the OData service that exposes your AI capabilities. CAP generates a fully compliant OData V4 API from this definition — no manual API implementation required.

// srv/ai-service.cds
using ai.sap.service from '../db/schema';
 
service AISapService @(path: '/ai-service') {
 
    // Expose query log as read-only OData entity
    @readonly
    entity QueryLog as projection on ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog;
 
    // Expose knowledge base docs with full CRUD
    entity KnowledgeDocs as projection on ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc;
 
    // Custom action: AI-powered master data search
    action searchMasterData(query: String, maxResults: Integer)
        returns array of {
            materialNumber: String;
            description:   String;
            similarity:    Decimal;
            aiSummary:     String;
        };
 
    // Custom action: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    action askKnowledgeBase(question: String, category: String)
        returns {
            answer:   String;
            sources:  array of { docId: String; title: String; score: Decimal; };
            modelUsed: String;
        };
}

CAP generates the full OData V4 metadata document, entity CRUD operations, and batch processing from the .cds definition automatically. The custom actions (searchMasterData, askKnowledgeBase) are where your AI logic runs — implemented in the service handler in the next step.

STEP 3: AI CORE SERVICE BINDING 

Binding AI Core and HANA Cloud to the CAP Application

BTP Service Instances

Create and bind the required BTP service instances to your CAP application. In BTP Cloud Foundry, service binding injects credentials into VCAP_SERVICES at runtime — the SAP AI SDK reads these automatically.

# Create AI Core service instance (Extended plan required for Gen AI Hub)
cf create-service aicore extended ai-core-instance
 
# Create HANA Cloud service instance
cf create-service hana-cloud hana ai-hana-instance
 
# Create Destination service instance (for Integration Suite connectivity)
cf create-service destination lite ai-destination-instance

MTA Deployment Descriptor (mta.yaml)

The mta.yaml file defines your CAP application's deployment configuration and service bindings for BTP. CAP generates a base version with cds add cf-manifest — extend it to include AI Core binding:

# mta.yaml — key sections for AI Core binding
modules:
  - name: ai-sap-service-srv
    type: nodejs
    path: gen/srv
    parameters:
      buildpack: nodejs_buildpack
      memory: 512M
    requires:
      - name: ai-core-instance      # AI Core binding
      - name: ai-hana-instance      # HANA Cloud binding
      - name: ai-destination-instance
 
resources:
  - name: ai-core-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: aicore
      service-plan: extended
  - name: ai-hana-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: hana-cloud
      service-plan: hana
  - name: ai-destination-instance
    type: org.cloudfoundry.managed-service
    parameters:
      service: destination
      service-plan: lite

The AI Core Extended plan is required for Generative AI Hub access. The Standard plan provides AI Core infrastructure for custom model training but does not include Generative AI Hub model access. Verify your BTP subaccount has the Extended plan entitlement before deploying.

STEP 4: AI LOGIC IN CAP SERVICE HANDLERS 

Implementing AI Logic in CAP Service Handlers

The service handler file (srv/ai-service.js) is where your AI logic runs. CAP routes incoming OData action calls to the handler functions registered here. The SAP AI SDK handles AI Core authentication automatically using the VCAP_SERVICES credentials injected at runtime.

  ▶  Use Case 1: AI-Powered Master Data Search 

searchMasterData Action Handler

This handler receives a natural language search query, converts it to a vector embedding using the SAP embedding model, retrieves similar master data records from the HANA vector store built in Part 4, then generates an AI summary for each result. The response is returned as a structured OData collection.

// srv/ai-service.js
const cds = require('@sap/cds');
const { ChatOpenAI } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
const { OpenAIEmbeddings } = require('@sap-ai-sdk/langchain');
 
module.exports = cds.service.impl(async function() {
 
    // Initialize AI models — SDK reads AI Core credentials from VCAP_SERVICES
    const llm = new ChatOpenAI({
        modelName: 'gpt-4o',
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    const embeddings = new OpenAIEmbeddings({
        modelName: 'text-embedding-ada-002',
        // SAP embedding model via AI Core
        resourceGroup: 'default'
    });
 
    // Use Case 1: AI-powered master data search
    this.on('searchMasterData', async (req) => {
        const { query, maxResults = 5 } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Generate embedding for the search query
        const queryEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(query);
 
        // Step 2: Similarity search in HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const results = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP ${maxResults}
                SOURCE_ID as materialNumber,
                CHUNK_TEXT as description,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(queryEmbedding)}'))
                AS similarity
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'MASTER_DATA'
            ORDER BY similarity DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Generate AI summary for each result
        const enriched = await Promise.all(results.map(async (row) => {
            const summary = await llm.invoke([{
                role: 'system',
                content: 'Summarize this SAP material record in one sentence. Be specific and technical.'
            }, {
                role: 'user',
                content: `Material: ${row.description}`
            }]);
 
            return {
                materialNumber: row.materialNumber,
                description:   row.description,
                similarity:    parseFloat(row.similarity.toFixed(4)),
                aiSummary:     summary.content
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 4: Log query to HANA for audit trail
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query,
            response:       JSON.stringify(enriched),
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'MASTER_DATA',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return enriched;
    });

  ▶  Use Case 2: RAG-Backed Knowledge Base Q&A 

askKnowledgeBase Action Handler

This handler implements the full RAG pipeline from Part 4 inside a CAP service action — making it callable as an OData action from S/4HANA, Fiori, or Integration Suite. The category parameter enables scoped retrieval, limiting similarity search to a specific knowledge domain.

    // Use Case 2: RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A
    this.on('askKnowledgeBase', async (req) => {
        const { question, category } = req.data;
 
        // Step 1: Embed the question
        const questionEmbedding = await embeddings.embedQuery(question);
 
        // Step 2: Retrieve relevant chunks from HANA vector store
        const db = await cds.connect.to('db');
        const categoryFilter = category ? `AND JSON_VALUE(METADATA, '$.category') = '${category}'` : '';
 
        const chunks = await db.run(`
            SELECT TOP 5
                SOURCE_ID as docId,
                CHUNK_TEXT as content,
                METADATA,
                COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}'))
                AS score
            FROM AI_VECTOR_STORE.DOCUMENT_EMBEDDINGS
            WHERE SOURCE_TYPE = 'KB'
            AND COSINE_SIMILARITY(EMBEDDING, TO_REAL_VECTOR('${JSON.stringify(questionEmbedding)}')) > 0.75
            ${categoryFilter}
            ORDER BY score DESC
        `);
 
        // Step 3: Assemble retrieved context
        const context = chunks.map(c =>
            `[Source: ${c.docId} | Score: ${c.score.toFixed(3)}]\n${c.content}`
        ).join('\n\n---\n\n');
 
        // Step 4: Generate grounded answer — ONLY from retrieved context
        const answer = await llm.invoke([{
            role: 'system',
            content: `You are an SAP expert assistant.
Answer questions using ONLY the context provided.
If the context does not contain enough information, say so explicitly.
Do not use general SAP knowledge — only the provided context.
Always reference the source document when citing specific information.`
        }, {
            role: 'user',
            content: `CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}`
        }]);
 
        // Step 5: Build response with sources
        const sources = await Promise.all(chunks.map(async (c) => {
            const doc = await SELECT.one.from('ai.sap.service.KnowledgeBaseDoc')
                .where({ ID: c.docId });
            return {
                docId: c.docId,
                title: doc?.title || c.docId,
                score: parseFloat(c.score.toFixed(4))
            };
        }));
 
        // Step 6: Log to HANA for audit
        await db.run(INSERT.into('ai.sap.service.AIQueryLog').entries({
            ID:             cds.utils.uuid(),
            query:          question,
            response:       answer.content,
            modelUsed:      'gpt-4o',
            sourceType:     'KNOWLEDGE_BASE',
            createdAt:      new Date().toISOString(),
            createdBy:      req.user?.id || 'system'
        }));
 
        return {
            answer:    answer.content,
            sources,
            modelUsed: 'gpt-4o'
        };
    });
 
});

Every AI query is logged to HANA via the AIQueryLog entity. This audit trail is not optional in enterprise SAP deployments — it provides the traceability that compliance, security, and governance teams require. The log captures the query, response, model used, source type, timestamp, and user — everything needed for a complete audit record.

STEP 5: SAP INTEGRATION SUITE CONNECTIVITY 

Connecting via SAP Integration Suite

SAP Integration Suite acts as the orchestration layer between your CAP AI service and SAP backend systems. Rather than S/4HANA calling your CAP service directly, Integration Suite manages the flow — applying transformation, error handling, retry logic, and monitoring across the end-to-end process.

Destination Configuration

Create a BTP Destination pointing to your deployed CAP service. Integration Suite uses this destination to route calls to your AI endpoints:

# BTP Destination configuration for CAP AI service
Name:             AI_SAP_SERVICE
Type:             HTTP
URL:              https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
Authentication:   OAuth2ClientCredentials
Client ID:        <CAP application client ID>
Token Service URL: https://<subdomain>.authentication.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token

Integration Suite iFlow Pattern

A typical AI-augmented process flow in Integration Suite follows this pattern — S/4HANA triggers a business event, Integration Suite calls your CAP AI service, the AI response enriches or routes the downstream process:

S/4HANA event →	iFlow trigger →	CAP AI action call → AI response → Process routing

Example use case: A new material is created in S/4HANA. Integration Suite captures the MaterialCreated event, calls the CAP searchMasterData action to check for similar existing materials, and routes the process based on the AI similarity score — flagging potential duplicates for review before the material is activated.

When calling CAP OData actions from Integration Suite iFlows, use the HTTP Receiver adapter with OAuth2 authentication pointing to the BTP Destination created above. Do not embed AI Core credentials directly in iFlow configurations — all AI credential management should remain within the CAP service layer and its BTP service bindings.

STEP 6: CLOUD FOUNDRY DEPLOYMENT 

Deploying to BTP Cloud Foundry

Build and Deploy

# Build the CAP project for production
cds build --production
 
# Build the MTA archive
mbt build
 
# Deploy to BTP Cloud Foundry
cf login -a https://api.cf.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
cf deploy mta_archives/ai-sap-service_1.0.0.mtar

Verify Deployment

# Check application status
cf apps
 
# Check service bindings are correctly attached
cf services
 
# Tail logs to confirm AI Core connection on startup
cf logs ai-sap-service-srv --recent

Test the Deployed OData Actions

Once deployed, test your AI actions via the OData endpoint. The CAP service exposes a metadata document at /ai-service/$metadata — verify it loads correctly before testing the actions:

# Test master data search action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/searchMasterData' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query": "hydraulic pump assembly", "maxResults": 3}'
 
# Test knowledge base Q&A action
curl -X POST 'https://ai-sap-service-srv.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/ai-service/askKnowledgeBase' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"question": "How should I handle delta load failures?", "category": "HANA"}'

A successful deployment produces two working OData actions callable from any SAP system or external client — AI-powered master data search and RAG-backed knowledge base Q&A — with full audit logging to HANA Cloud, governed AI Core authentication, and Integration Suite connectivity for S/4HANA process orchestration.

PRODUCTION DESIGN CONSIDERATIONS 

What to Get Right Before Going Live

 

Consideration

Guidance

AI Core credentials

Never hardcode credentials in CAP service handlers. Always use VCAP_SERVICES binding via mta.yaml. Hardcoded credentials are a security violation and will fail rotation.

HANA vector store alignment

The HANA Cloud instance bound to your CAP application must be the same instance containing the AI_VECTOR_STORE schema from Part 4. Mismatched instances is the most common deployment error.

Similarity score threshold

Keep the 0.75 COSINE_SIMILARITY threshold from Part 4 in your CAP handler. Removing it returns low-relevance chunks that degrade answer quality and increase token consumption.

Audit log privacy

The AIQueryLog entity captures user queries. Review your data privacy policy before deploying — in some organizations, logging user query content requires explicit data handling disclosure.

Integration Suite authentication

iFlows calling your CAP service must use OAuth2 via BTP Destination — not basic auth. Basic auth against CAP OData endpoints is deprecated in BTP Cloud Foundry environments.

CAP multitenancy

If your CAP application serves multiple SAP tenants, enable CAP's built-in multitenancy (cds add multitenancy) before deploying. Single-tenant deployments cannot be retrofitted for multitenancy without significant rework.

What's Next in This Series
