HTTP 409 Conflict means the resource is in a state that doesn't allow the requested operation. A resource group in Provisioning state is actively being worked on by the platform — deleting it while provisioning is in progress is not permitted.

The Fix

Wait for the provisioning process to either complete (status: PROVISIONED) or fail (status: ERROR) before attempting to delete. If the status has been Provisioning for over 15 minutes without changing, the platform may be unresponsive — in this case wait 30 minutes before retrying the delete.

If the delete still fails after the status shows ERROR, use the curl DELETE command rather than the UI — the API is more reliable for deleting failed resource groups:

curl -X DELETE "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups/YOUR_GROUP_ID" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"
ERROR 11 OF 12  
ERROR 11    Orchestration vs foundation-models scenario mismatch    Empty response or wrong endpoint format 
What You See

Your deployments list returns a deployment with status RUNNING, but when you call the inference endpoint using the standard chat/completions format, you get an empty response or a format error.

What Causes It

SAP AI Core has two distinct deployment scenarios — foundation-models and orchestration. They use different inference endpoint formats and different request body structures.

  • foundation-models deployments use /chat/completions with a standard OpenAI-compatible request body including a model field
  • orchestration deployments use /completion with an orchestration_config wrapper structure — a completely different format

If your deployment was created under the orchestration scenario (check the scenarioId field in the deployments list response) but you're calling it with the foundation-models format, the request either fails silently or returns a format error.

How to Check Your Scenario

In your deployments list response, find the scenarioId field for your deployment:

{"id":"d01e9590...","scenarioId":"orchestration",...}  ← orchestration deployment
{"id":"df600c1a...","scenarioId":"foundation-models",...}  ← foundation-models deployment
The Fix — foundation-models deployment (standard format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}],"max_tokens":100}"
The Fix — orchestration deployment (orchestration format)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_ID/completion" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"orchestration_config":{"module_configurations":{"llm_module_config":{"model_name":"anthropic--claude-3-5-sonnet","model_params":{"max_tokens":500}}}},"messages_history":[],"template":{"template":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello"}]}}"

For most use cases — direct model access for Python applications and RAG pipelines — the foundation-models scenario is the correct choice. Orchestration is for SAP's managed prompt orchestration service with additional capabilities like content filtering and grounding. If you want direct foundation model access, create your configuration with scenarioId: foundation-models.

ERROR 12 OF 12  
ERROR 12    AI Launchpad showing wrong instance    Configurations visible but inference fails 
What You See

You create a configuration and deployment in AI Launchpad and can see it listed. But when you use curl with your service key credentials, the deployment is either not found in the list or returns 404 on inference.

What Causes It

AI Launchpad is a separate BTP subscription that can connect to multiple AI Core instances. The workspace selector in the Launchpad header controls which instance you're viewing and managing. If you have subscriptions to AI Launchpad in multiple BTP subaccounts, each may open to a different AI Core connection by default.

The result: you create a deployment while connected to instance A in Launchpad, but your service key belongs to instance B. The Launchpad URL in the browser contains the subaccount subdomain — this is the clearest indicator of which Launchpad instance you're using.

How to Diagnose It

Check the browser URL when you're in AI Launchpad. The subdomain in the URL identifies which BTP subaccount (and therefore which AI Launchpad subscription) you're using:

https://YOUR-SUBDOMAIN.ai-launchpad.prod.us-east-1.aws.ai-prod.cloud.sap/...

Compare YOUR-SUBDOMAIN against the subdomain in your service key's url field. If they don't match — you're in the wrong Launchpad.

The Fix
  1. Go to BTP Cockpit → the subaccount that contains your AI Core service instance
  2. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions → SAP AI Launchpad → Open
  3. This opens the Launchpad connected to that specific subaccount's AI Core instance
  4. Create your configuration and deployment here — it will be visible to your service key credentials

Bookmark the AI Launchpad URL for each subaccount separately. When you have multiple subaccounts, it's easy to open the wrong Launchpad instance by navigating directly to the URL without checking which subaccount it belongs to.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Deployment and Configuration Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

404 on RUNNING deployment

Token expired or wrong resource group header

Refresh token and confirm AI-Resource-Group header matches deployment's group

404 despite correct token and group

Deployment lives in different AI Core instance than service key

Check instance_id label in resource group — get service key for the correct instance

Resource group stuck in Provisioning

Plan restriction or platform-level issue

Wait 5 min, retry via curl, raise CA-ML-AIP ticket if persists beyond 15 min

409 on resource group delete

Resource group still in active Provisioning state

Wait for PROVISIONED or ERROR status before deleting

Empty response or format error on inference

Calling orchestration deployment with foundation-models format

Check scenarioId in deployment list — use /completion endpoint for orchestration

Deployment in Launchpad but not in curl list

Launchpad connected to wrong subaccount's AI Core instance

Open Launchpad from correct BTP subaccount — check browser URL subdomain

THE WORKING INFERENCE CALL 

Complete Working Model Inference Call

Once all twelve errors are resolved, this is the complete working inference call for a foundation-models deployment. All values in capitals are replaced with your actual values:

# Step 1: Get fresh token (valid 1 hour)
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"
 
# Step 2: List deployments to confirm ID and resource group
curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/lm/deployments" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default"
 
# Step 3: Call the model
curl -X POST "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/inference/deployments/YOUR_DEPLOYMENT_ID/chat/completions" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN" ^
  -H "AI-Resource-Group: default" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" ^
  -d "{"model":"YOUR_MODEL_NAME","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Hello, are you working?"}],"max_tokens":100}"

A successful response contains choices[0].message.content with the model's reply. Your end-to-end connection is proven. From here — move to the generative-ai-hub-sdk for Python integration (covered in the Generative AI Hub series) which handles token management, deployment ID resolution, and model routing automatically.

entry.title: Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the RPT-1 API Token
entry.content:
In my previous blog about experimenting with RPT-1, we explored how to try out using the RPT-1 playground version. Now, let’s see how this can be done programmatically. Please check the  SAP Cloud SDK for Python for RPT-1 native APIs. We will first use request library to call the API directly.

Prerequisites:

  1. An API key for the service – you can get it from the ‘Your API Token’ section at https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs.

 

 

 

We will use the same .csv file used previously for the Freight Orders with ‘[PREDICT]’ tag.

RPT-1 API accept json format. So, lets first convert .csv file to json format.

#Read csv and convert to json
import csv
import json
def csv_to_json(csv_file_path):

    data = []
    with open(csv_file_path, encoding='utf-8') as csv_file:
       reader = csv.DictReader(csv_file) 
        for row in reader:
            data.append(row
    final_payload = {
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": "Carrier",
                    "prediction_placeholder": "[PREDICT]"
                }
            ]
        },
        "index_column": "Document" ,
        "rows": data         
    }
    print(f"Done! {len(data)} records converted to json")
    return final_payload

 

Prepare the request parameters

access_token = "<<Your access token here>>" #Use .env for secure storage
url = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"

Now call the API

import requests
import json
headers = {
    "Authorization": f"Bearer {access_token}",
    "Content-Type": "application/json",
    "Accept": "application/json",
}
final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv')
response = requests.post(url, headers=headers, json=final_payload, timeout=120)
response.raise_for_status()
data_pred = response.json()
preds = data_pred.get("prediction", [])
print(json.dumps(preds, indent=2, ensure_ascii=False))

The result would look like this.

{
  "id": "ba15b9bd-8cde-4eee-b858-0329f1b48e11",
  "metadata": {
    "num_columns": 38,
    "num_predictions": 1,
    "num_query_rows": 1,
    "num_rows": 334
  },
  "predictions": [
    {
      "Carrier": [
        {
          "confidence": 0.46,
          "confidence_interval": null,
          "prediction": "MM_CAR_01"
        }
      ],
      "Document": 6100000600
    }
  ],
  "status": {
    "code": 0,
    "message": "ok"
  }
}

If you have RPT-1 deployment in SAP AI Launchpad, then you can use native API call.

import os
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.models import RPTRequest, PredictionConfig, TargetColumn
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.native.sap.client import RPTClient
#Get the credentials from the AI Core instance in your BTP sub-account
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "<<AICORE_AUTH_URL>>oauth/token"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_ID>>"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "<<AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET>>"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "<<AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP>>"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "<<AICORE_BASE_URL>>/v2"

final_payload = csv_to_json('sample.csv', target_columns=["Carrier"])

client = RPTClient()
body = RPTRequest(
            prediction_config=PredictionConfig(
                target_columns=[
                    TargetColumn(name="Carrier", task_type="classification")
                ]),
            rows=final_payload
        )
response = client.predict(body=body, model_name="sap-rpt-1-small")
print(response.predictions)

This example shows using Python, but this can be done in ABAP as well using HTTP request call (such as a save process controller strategy method in SAP TM). 

What you learned

  • How to shape tabular data for SAP RPT‑1 with a clear prediction_config and index_column
  • How to invoke the predict API(using request call and native API) securely and read confidence‑scored outputs
  • We used 'predicting carrier' just as an example and to understand the usage. You can think of any other use cases which require prediction from minimal data set which doesn't require complex classic ML training.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent

Autonomous reasoner. Given a goal, the agent decides which tools to use, in what order, based on what it observes at each step. The sequence is not predetermined.

Assess an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness — the agent decides which systems to query, what to look for, and how to structure findings based on what it discovers.

The key differentiator is decision-making under uncertainty. Pipelines and skills are deterministic — given the same input, they always follow the same path. Agents are adaptive — they observe, reason, and decide what to do next based on what they find.

When to Use Each

Use a pipeline when the steps are known upfront and the output structure is fixed. Use a skill when the task is focused and the API call is well-defined. Use an agent when the task requires exploration, multi-step reasoning, or when the path to completion depends on what the agent discovers along the way.

Scenario

Use

SAP Example

Known steps, fixed output structure

Prompt pipeline

Exception log triage, document extraction

Single focused task, defined API

Joule skill

Material lookup, KB question, badge creation

Goal-directed, path unknown upfront

Agent

Landscape assessment, readiness analysis

Multi-step with dependencies on findings

Agent

Data quality sweep, migration risk scoring

Needs human approval at checkpoints

Agent with human-in-the-loop

Transport risk assessment, SoD conflict review

Agents are not always better than pipelines. They are more expensive (more LLM calls), less predictable (the path varies), and harder to debug. Use agents specifically when the task requires adaptive reasoning. For everything else, pipelines and skills are faster, cheaper, and more reliable.

PART B: ARCHITECTURE — THE AGENTIC SAP STACK 

The Complete LangGraph + AI Core Architecture for SAP Agents

Production SAP agents require four layers working together. LangGraph provides the agent orchestration framework. SAP AI Core provides governed foundation model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. BTP Cloud Foundry provides the deployment and runtime environment.

LangGraph Agent → AI Core (foundation model) → SAP Tools (HANA, APIs) → HANA vector store → Findings output
Why LangGraph for SAP Agents

LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework built on LangChain. It models agent execution as a directed graph — nodes are actions or decisions, edges are transitions. This architecture makes it uniquely suited for SAP workloads for three reasons:

  • State persistence — LangGraph maintains agent state explicitly across steps. For SAP assessments that query multiple systems and accumulate findings, state persistence is essential.
  • Conditional routing — edges in the graph can be conditional. An agent can take different paths based on what it finds — escalate a critical finding, skip a check if a prerequisite fails, loop back for more data if confidence is low.
  • Human-in-the-loop support — LangGraph has native support for pausing execution and waiting for human approval before proceeding. For SAP processes where autonomous action carries risk, this is not optional.
The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reason + Act) is the foundational agent pattern used throughout this series. The agent alternates between two steps:

  • Reason — the model thinks about what it knows and what it needs to do next
  • Act — the model calls a tool (a function, an API, a database query) and observes the result

This cycle continues until the agent has enough information to produce a final answer or reaches a defined stopping condition. For SAP workloads, the tools are SAP-specific: HANA queries, RFC calls, AI Core model invocations, vector store retrievals.

Goal input → Reason: what do I know? → Act: call SAP tool → Observe result → Reason: what next? → Final findings
Agent State Schema for SAP Assessments

Every LangGraph agent requires an explicit state schema. For SAP assessment agents, the state carries the accumulated findings, queries run, systems checked, and confidence scores across all steps:

# Agent state schema — carries context across all reasoning steps

from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class SAPAssessmentState(TypedDict):

    goal:              str          # What the agent is trying to assess

    system_info:       dict         # SAP system metadata collected so far

    findings:          List[dict]   # Accumulated findings with severity

    queries_run:       List[str]    # Queries executed — for audit trail

    tools_used:        List[str]    # Tools called — for transparency

    confidence_score:  float        # Overall confidence in findings

    next_action:       str          # What the agent decided to do next

    human_review_needed: bool       # Flag for human-in-the-loop checkpoint

    final_report:      Optional[str] # Final output when complete
PART C: YOUR FIRST SAP AGENT — REACT WITH LANGGRAPH 

Building a ReAct Agent for SAP Data Assessment

This first agent demonstrates the complete ReAct pattern on a focused SAP task: given a HANA table name, autonomously assess its data quality — checking volume, null rates, duplicate rates, and anomalies — and produce a structured findings report. This is the foundation pattern that the more complex agents in Parts 2–6 build on.

Step 1 — Install Dependencies
# Install LangGraph and SAP AI SDK
pip install langgraph langchain-core generative-ai-hub-sdk hdbcli
Step 2 — Define SAP Tools

Tools are the functions the agent can call. Each tool has a name, description (used by the model to decide when to call it), and implementation. For SAP agents, tools wrap HANA queries and AI Core calls:

# tools.py — SAP tools available to the agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json
 
# HANA connection — credentials from environment or .aicore-config.json
def get_hana_connection():
    return hdb.connect(
        address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'],
        port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'],
        encrypt=True
    )
 
@tool
def get_table_volume(table_name: str) -> str:
    """Get the row count and basic metadata for an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table_name}')
    count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
    return json.dumps({'table': table_name, 'row_count': count})
 
@tool
def get_null_rate(table_name: str, column_name: str) -> str:
    """Calculate the null rate for a specific column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            COUNT(*) as total,
            SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as nulls,
            ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
                / COUNT(*), 2) as null_rate_pct
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'column': column_name,
        'total': row[0], 'nulls': row[1], 'null_rate_pct': float(row[2])
    })
 
@tool
def get_duplicate_rate(table_name: str, key_column: str) -> str:
    """Identify duplicate records based on a key column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT {key_column}) as duplicate_count,
               COUNT(*) as total_count
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'key_column': key_column,
        'duplicate_count': row[0], 'total_count': row[1],
        'duplicate_rate_pct': round(100.0 * row[0] / row[1], 2) if row[1] > 0 else 0
    })
 
@tool
def generate_assessment_report(findings: str) -> str:
    """Generate a structured data quality assessment report from accumulated findings."""
    from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
    llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
    response = llm([
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP data quality expert.
        Generate a structured assessment report from the findings provided.
        Include: Executive Summary, Critical Issues, Recommendations.
        Be specific and actionable.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from findings:\n{findings}')
    ])
    return response.content

Step 3 — Build the LangGraph Agent

# agent.py — LangGraph ReAct agent for SAP data quality assessment
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools import get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report
 
# Initialize foundation model via SAP AI Core
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Register SAP tools the agent can use
tools = [get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report]
 
# System prompt — defines agent behavior and boundaries
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP data quality assessment agent.
Your goal is to autonomously assess the data quality of SAP HANA tables.

For each assessment:
1. Check the table volume first
2. Check null rates for key columns
3. Check duplicate rates on primary key columns
4. Flag any issue where null_rate > 5% or duplicate_rate > 0.1% as HIGH severity
5. Generate a structured findings report when assessment is complete

Always explain your reasoning before calling a tool.
Always summarize what you found after each tool call.
Stop when you have assessed all requested dimensions.'''
 
# Create the ReAct agent — LangGraph handles the reason/act loop
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Step 4 — Run the Agent

# Run the agent on an SAP table assessment task
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the data quality of table MARA (SAP material master).
        Check volume, null rate on MTART and MATKL columns,
        and duplicate rate on MATNR. Generate a findings report.
    ''')]
})
 
# Print the agent's final response
print(result['messages'][-1].content)

The agent will reason through the task step by step — calling get_table_volume first, then get_null_rate for each column, then get_duplicate_rate, and finally generate_assessment_report with all accumulated findings. The reasoning trace is visible in the messages — each HumanMessage and AIMessage shows exactly what the agent thought and did.

What the Agent Output Looks Like

The agent produces a structured findings report with:

  • Executive Summary — overall data quality score and critical issues count
  • Findings per dimension — volume, nulls, duplicates with actual values
  • Severity classification — Critical, High, Medium, Low per finding
  • Specific recommendations — actionable fixes referencing actual column names and values

This output is the foundation for the migration assessment agent in Part 2, which extends this pattern across multiple SAP tables and produces a full landscape readiness report.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT AGENT — EXECUTION TRACE      ║
║         Table: MARA (Material Master — General Data)             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[THOUGHT] I need to begin by checking the volume of the MARA table to
understand the scale of the assessment before evaluating data quality
dimensions.

[ACTION] get_table_volume("MARA")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "row_count": 847293}

[THOUGHT] The table contains 847,293 material master records. This is
a significant volume — quality issues even at low percentages will
affect thousands of records. I'll now check null rates starting with
MTART (Material Type), a critical classification field.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MTART")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MTART", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 169, "null_rate_pct": 0.02}

[THOUGHT] MTART null rate is 0.02% — acceptable. 169 records without
a material type is low risk. Moving to MATKL (Material Group), which
drives procurement categorization and reporting.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MATKL")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MATKL", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 73,969, "null_rate_pct": 8.73}

[THOUGHT] MATKL null rate is 8.73% — this exceeds the 5% threshold
and is flagged HIGH severity. 73,969 materials without a material group
will cause issues in procurement reporting, spend analytics, and any
AI classification pipeline that relies on MATKL for categorization.
This needs to be highlighted in the final report.

[ACTION] get_duplicate_rate("MARA", "MATNR")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "key_column": "MATNR",
               "duplicate_count": 0, "total_count": 847293,
               "duplicate_rate_pct": 0.0}

[THOUGHT] MATNR (Material Number) has zero duplicates — primary key
integrity is intact. I now have sufficient findings to generate the
structured assessment report.

[ACTION] generate_assessment_report(findings)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT REPORT
Table: MARA — Material Master General Data
Assessment Date: 2026-03-28 | Agent: SAP Data Quality Agent v1.0
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────
Overall Data Quality Score: 74/100 — ATTENTION REQUIRED
Records Assessed: 847,293
Critical Issues: 0  |  High Severity: 1  |  Low Severity: 2

One high-severity data quality issue was identified that will impact
procurement reporting, spend analytics, and downstream AI pipelines
that rely on material group classification. Immediate remediation is
recommended before any S/4HANA migration or AI workload deployment.

FINDINGS
────────
┌──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Dimension    │ Column       │ Value         │ Threshold│ Severity │
├──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Volume       │ —            │ 847,293 rows  │ —        │ OK       │
│ Null Rate    │ MTART        │ 0.02% (169)   │ < 5%     │ LOW      │
│ Null Rate    │ MATKL        │ 8.73% (73,969)│ < 5%     │ HIGH ⚠   │
│ Duplicates   │ MATNR        │ 0.0%          │ < 0.1%   │ LOW      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

HIGH SEVERITY FINDING — MATKL (Material Group) Null Rate: 8.73%
73,969 of 847,293 material records are missing a Material Group
assignment. Impact areas:
  • Procurement spend reporting by category will be incomplete
  • AI-based material classification will produce unreliable results
  • S/4HANA migration data validation will flag these records

RECOMMENDATIONS
───────────────
1. [IMMEDIATE] Run MATKL gap analysis — extract all MATNR records
   where MATKL is null. Group by MTART to identify which material
   types are most affected. This scopes the remediation effort.

2. [SHORT-TERM] Assign Material Groups using the AI classification
   pipeline (Part 2 of Generative AI Hub series) — embed existing
   classified materials as reference examples and classify the
   73,969 unclassified records using RAG-augmented classification.

3. [BEFORE MIGRATION] Validate MATKL completeness reaches < 2%
   null rate before proceeding with any S/4HANA migration data
   load. Include this check in your migration go/no-go criteria.

4. [PROCESS] Add MATKL as a required field in material master
   creation workflows in MM01 to prevent future gaps.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Agent completed assessment in 4 reasoning steps | 4 tool calls
Confidence Score: 0.91 | Human Review Required: NO
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SERIES 2 ROADMAP 

What This Series Builds Across 6 Parts

 

Part

Title

What It Builds

Part 1

Concepts, Architecture, First ReAct Agent

Agent vs skill vs pipeline, LangGraph + AI Core stack, data quality ReAct agent on HANA

Part 2

SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent

Multi-tool agent that analyzes SAP landscape, scores migration readiness, produces findings report

Part 3

SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

Autonomous root cause analysis — agent queries logs, identifies patterns, recommends actions without human triage

Part 4

SAP Master Data Quality Agent

Detects data issues across master data domains, prioritizes fixes, generates remediation plan

Part 5

S/4HANA Readiness Agent

Assesses custom code and configuration against clean core principles, flags migration blockers

Part 6

Multi-Agent Orchestration

All four agents working together — an orchestrator agent delegates to specialized sub-agents and synthesizes a unified SAP landscape report

What's Next — Part 2

Part 2 builds the SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent — extending the ReAct pattern from a single table to a full landscape assessment across multiple systems, with conditional routing based on findings and a scored readiness report as output. The agent decides autonomously which systems to query, what to look for, and how to weight findings based on migration impact.

If you work through the ReAct agent in this article and want to extend it to your own SAP tables — drop a comment with your use case. The pattern generalizes across any HANA table and any assessment dimension. The tool definitions are the only thing that changes.

entry.title: Your AI Models Just Got a Hardware Upgrade
entry.content:
Co-Author: Lukas Pätz

TL;DR

SAP AI Core (the engine) now supports flexible instance types — pick the exact CPU or GPU your workload needs
New instance types deliver up to 4.5–5x the compute performance of old resource plans
Access the latest GPUs (L4, L40S, H100) without waiting for SAP to package them into plans
Your existing resource plans still work — instance types are opt-in where they help

Start Here: SAP Note 3660109

Why This Matters

If you're running your custom AI models on SAP's Generative AI Hub (technical service SAP AI Core), this one's for you.

We've heard the feedback: newer GPUs are available from hyperscalers, but you can't use them yet because SAP hasn't released a matching resource plan. That lag is frustrating — especially when the difference between a V100 and an H100 isn't incremental. It's generational. We're talking 4.5–5x the compute performance for the right workloads. An inference call that takes 8 seconds on older hardware might finish in under 1 second on newer chips. That's the gap between "possible in theory" and "shipping in production."

Starting now, that gap is closing.
 

What Changed: Resource Plans → Instance Types

Think of the old model like a set menu at a restaurant. Curated, predictable, but limited to what the chef decided to offer.

Instance types are the shift to à la carte. You see the full range of CPUs and GPUs available across cloud providers, and you pick exactly what your workload needs — from a lightweight CPU for preprocessing to an H100 for training large models.

Resource plans aren't going away. They still work. But instance types are now the primary path for getting the newest, most capable hardware into your deployments — faster, and with finer control than the old model allowed. We highly recommend to leverage instance types going forward (only available in the "extended" service plan, now be the right time to migrate from standard to extended)

Here's the point: you tell us what your model needs; we make sure the right compute is there. Bon appétit.

What You Actually Get

A quick clarification: If you're using pre-integrated foundation models through SAP's Generative AI Hub — GPT-5.2, Claude 4.6 Opus or Gemini — this update doesn't directly affect you. Those models are already managed and optimized; you just call the API.

Instance types matter when you're running your own models: custom fine-tuned models, your proprietary models (like team-built solutions), embedding models for RAG pipelines, or specialized computer vision and industry models. That's when hardware choice becomes your concern — and when better hardware translates directly into better outcomes.

Faster inference, faster training

This isn't about spec sheets. It's about what becomes possible with the models you've built.

Custom models that were borderline viable for real-time use — domain-specific classifiers, fine-tuned embedding models, proprietary prediction models — become clearly viable when you can run them on L40S instead of T4. Training jobs that took days can finish in hours on H100. Features your team considered out of reach might now fit inside your existing budget.

L4 and L40S chips deliver substantially higher throughput than T4, at lower cost per inference call. H100 provides order-of-magnitude gains over V100 for training workloads. If you're pushing the limits of what's feasible with custom models, this matters.

Better cost efficiency — without re-architecting

Faster hardware means less compute time per job, which means lower cost. Simple as that.

Instance types also let you right-size. You stop paying for a resource plan heavier than you need. The result: better cost-per-output without touching your model or application logic.

 

New hardware when it ships — not months later

When hyperscalers release the next GPU generation, you won't be waiting for SAP to define a new resource plan. Instance types let us surface new hardware significantly faster. Your infrastructure stays current.

Multi-cloud, multi-region resilience

Instance types are available across cloud providers and regions. Deploy closer to your users or data. Meet compliance requirements. Reduce single-vendor dependency. Keep two or three validated alternatives per region and you're protected against capacity constraints.

Which Instance Should You Use?

 

WorkloadRecommended InstanceExamples
Orchestration, preprocessing, CPU-based MLCPU (AWS m7i, Azure Dv6, GCP n4)Data pipelines, API routing, lightweight model serving
Custom model inference (high throughput)L4 / L40S GPU (AWS g6, g6e)Fine-tuned classifiers, embedding models, custom vision models
Fine-tuning and training medium-to-large modelsA100 (GCP A2 Ultra)Domain-specific model fine-tuning, complex custom inference
Training large language modelsH100 (Azure NC H100 v5)Custom LLM training, proprietary foundation model development

Match the instance to your workload. Account for regional availability. Keep at least two alternatives validated per region.

Use the compute factors in SAP Note 3660109 or the SAP Cost Calculator to compare options on a consistent basis — each instance type maps to a compute factor in node-hours, which makes cost planning straightforward.

Migration: No Big Bang Required

Both options work today. You don't have to migrate everything at once. More details:

# This still works
ai.sap.com/resourcePlan: "infer.s"

# This is the new option — use where it helps
ai.sap.com/instanceType: "g6.4xlarge"

Start with the workloads where newer hardware will have the clearest impact — typically your highest-volume inference jobs or your most compute-heavy training runs. Everything else can stay on resource plans, but newer hardware might be more efficient.

Full specs, regional availability, and compute factors: SAP Note 3660109.

Staying Current

Hardware in AI is moving fast — faster than enterprise software typically keeps up. Instance types are how we keep you on the right side of that curve, without adding operational overhead to your team.

To stay informed:

  • Subscribe to SAP Note 3660109 — it's the live catalogue; new instance types show up here as they become available
  • Watch the What's New page for implementation guidance and new instance families

The Bottom Line

This isn't a small tweak. It's a deliberate shift to put more control in your hands — and to get it there faster.

We handle the complexity: evaluating new hardware, qualifying it, making it available at scale. You focus on what you're actually here to do: build AI solutions that matter.

The menu just expanded. Pick your instance — and dig in.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 2 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent

From a single ReAct agent to a full multi-tool LangGraph state machine with human-in-the-loop — assessing custom code health, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies to produce a scored S/4HANA migration readiness report.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 2 builds the SAP Migration Assessment Agent in two stages. Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct pattern into a single agent with human-in-the-loop that assesses all four migration dimensions — custom code, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies. Stage B evolves this into a LangGraph state machine with conditional routing, where the agent's path through the assessment changes based on what it finds. Both stages produce a scored migration readiness report with dimension-level findings.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Part 1 established the foundational concepts — agent vs skill vs pipeline, the ReAct pattern, LangGraph architecture, and a first working agent for SAP data quality assessment. If you haven't read Part 1, the agent state schema and tool definitions introduced there are the starting point for everything built here.

Part 2 applies those foundations to a real SAP migration use case — assessing an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness across four dimensions. This is the kind of assessment that currently takes SAP migration teams weeks of manual analysis. The agent does it autonomously, with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is produced.

WHY MIGRATION ASSESSMENT IS AN IDEAL AGENT USE CASE 

Why Migration Assessment Fits the Agent Pattern

S/4HANA migration assessment is one of the most time-consuming and inconsistently executed activities in SAP consulting. Two experienced consultants assessing the same landscape often produce different findings because the process is manual, undocumented, and dependent on tribal knowledge about what to look for.

It fits the agent pattern for three reasons:

  • The path is not predetermined — what the agent looks for in custom code depends on how much custom code exists. What it investigates in integrations depends on which adapters it finds. The assessment adapts to the landscape.
  • It requires multi-tool reasoning — no single query answers the readiness question. The agent must combine findings across custom code, data volume, configuration, and interfaces to produce a meaningful score.
  • Human review before action is natural — a migration readiness report should be reviewed by a consultant before being shared with a client. Human-in-the-loop isn't a constraint here, it's the correct workflow.

Assessment Dimension

What It Signals for Migration

Custom code health

Volume of Z-objects, modifications, and deprecated API usage — signals clean core compliance gap

Table complexity

Record volumes and growth rates across key business objects — signals data migration effort

Configuration footprint

Scope of customizing across modules — signals business process complexity and testing effort

Integration dependencies

Interface count, adapter types, and external system connections — signals technical migration risk

STAGE A: SINGLE AGENT WITH HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP 

Stage A — Extending the ReAct Pattern with Human-in-the-Loop

Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct agent into a migration assessment agent. The core pattern is the same — the agent reasons and acts in a loop using SAP-specific tools. Two things are added: all four assessment dimensions as tools, and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is generated.

Goal: assess landscape → Custom code check → Table complexity → Config footprint → Integration scan → Human review → Scored report
Assessment State Schema

Extend the SAPAssessmentState from Part 1 with migration-specific fields:

# migration_agent.py — extends Part 1 state schema
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class MigrationAssessmentState(TypedDict):
    goal:                str
    system_id:           str          # SAP system being assessed
    # Dimension findings
    custom_code:         Optional[dict]  # Z-objects, mods, deprecated APIs
    table_complexity:    Optional[dict]  # Volumes and growth rates
    config_footprint:    Optional[dict]  # Customizing scope per module
    integration_deps:    Optional[dict]  # Interface count and types
    # Scoring
    dimension_scores:    Dict[str, int]   # Score per dimension 0-100
    overall_score:       Optional[int]    # Weighted overall readiness score
    readiness_level:     Optional[str]    # HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / CRITICAL
    # Control
    findings:            List[dict]
    human_review_needed: bool
    human_approved:      bool
    final_report:        Optional[str]
Migration Assessment Tools
# tools/migration_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
                       user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
                       password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def assess_custom_code(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess custom code health — count Z-objects, modifications,
    and usage of deprecated APIs that block S/4HANA migration."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count Z-programs and includes
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as z_program_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS LIKE 'Z%' AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS')
    ''')
    z_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    # Count SAP standard modifications (risky for migration)
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as mod_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS NOT LIKE 'Z%'
        AND DEVCLASS NOT LIKE '$%'
        AND DEVCLASS != 'LOCAL'
        AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS', 'DOMA', 'DTEL')
    ''')
    mod_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    return json.dumps({
        'z_program_count': z_count,
        'modification_count': mod_count,
        'clean_core_risk': 'HIGH' if mod_count > 100 else
                           'MEDIUM' if mod_count > 20 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_table_complexity(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess data volume complexity across key business object tables."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Key tables that drive migration effort
    key_tables = ['BKPF', 'BSEG', 'VBAK', 'VBAP', 'EKKO',
                  'EKPO', 'MARA', 'KNA1', 'LFA1', 'AUFK']
    volumes = {}
 
    for table in key_tables:
        try:
            cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}')
            volumes[table] = cursor.fetchone()[0]
        except Exception:
            volumes[table] = 'unavailable'
 
    total_records = sum(v for v in volumes.values() if isinstance(v, int))
    return json.dumps({
        'table_volumes': volumes,
        'total_key_records': total_records,
        'volume_risk': 'HIGH' if total_records > 500_000_000 else
                       'MEDIUM' if total_records > 50_000_000 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_config_footprint(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess configuration and customizing footprint across SAP modules."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count customizing entries per application area
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT LEFT(TABNAME, 2) as module_prefix,
               COUNT(*) as custom_entry_count
        FROM DD02L
        WHERE TABCLASS = 'C'
        GROUP BY LEFT(TABNAME, 2)
        ORDER BY custom_entry_count DESC
        LIMIT 10
    ''')
    modules = {row[0]: row[1] for row in cursor.fetchall()}
    total_custom = sum(modules.values())
 
    return json.dumps({
        'customizing_by_module': modules,
        'total_customizing_entries': total_custom,
        'config_complexity': 'HIGH' if total_custom > 10000 else
                             'MEDIUM' if total_custom > 2000 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_integration_dependencies(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess interface and integration dependency footprint."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count RFC destinations (external system connections)
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT RFCTYPE, COUNT(*) as connection_count
        FROM RFCDES
        WHERE RFCTYPE != 'L'
        -- Exclude loopback connections
        GROUP BY RFCTYPE
    ''')
    rfc_connections = {row[0]: row[1] for row in cursor.fetchall()}
    total_rfc = sum(rfc_connections.values())
 
    return json.dumps({
        'rfc_connections_by_type': rfc_connections,
        'total_external_connections': total_rfc,
        'integration_risk': 'HIGH' if total_rfc > 50 else
                            'MEDIUM' if total_rfc > 15 else 'LOW'
    })
Stage A Agent — ReAct with Human-in-the-Loop
# Stage A: Single ReAct agent with human-in-the-loop checkpoint
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools.migration_tools import (assess_custom_code,
    assess_table_complexity, assess_config_footprint,
    assess_integration_dependencies)
 
# MemorySaver enables human-in-the-loop by persisting state
memory = MemorySaver()
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
tools = [
    assess_custom_code,
    assess_table_complexity,
    assess_config_footprint,
    assess_integration_dependencies
]
 
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP migration assessment agent.
Assess the S/4HANA migration readiness of the SAP system provided.

Follow this sequence:
1. Assess custom code health
2. Assess table complexity and data volumes
3. Assess configuration and customizing footprint
4. Assess integration and interface dependencies
5. Score each dimension 0-100 (100 = fully ready, 0 = critical blocker)
6. Calculate a weighted overall readiness score
7. Present findings summary and WAIT for human review before final report

Scoring weights:
  Custom code: 30%  |  Table complexity: 25%
  Configuration: 20%  |  Integrations: 25%

Readiness levels:
  80-100: HIGH — proceed with migration planning
  60-79:  MEDIUM — address findings before committing timeline
  40-59:  LOW — significant remediation required
  0-39:   CRITICAL — do not proceed without major remediation'''
 
# interrupt_before=['tools'] pauses agent for human review
# before it calls the final report generation tool
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    checkpointer=memory,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Running Stage A with Human Review

# Run the agent — it pauses for human review after dimension assessment
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
# Thread ID maintains state across the pause/resume cycle
config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': 'migration-assessment-001'}}
 
# Phase 1: Agent assesses all four dimensions
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the S/4HANA migration readiness of system PRD.
        Assess all four dimensions and present a summary for review.
    ''')]
}, config=config)
 
# Agent presents findings and waits — human reviews here
print('--- AGENT FINDINGS FOR REVIEW ---')
print(result['messages'][-1].content)
 
# Phase 2: Human approves and agent generates final scored report
human_input = input('Review complete. Approve report generation? (yes/no): ')
 
if human_input.lower() == 'yes':
    final = agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
            Findings approved. Generate the full scored migration
            readiness report with dimension scores, overall score,
            readiness level, and prioritized recommendations.
        ''')]
    }, config=config)
    print(final['messages'][-1].content)

The thread_id in config is what makes human-in-the-loop work. LangGraph persists the agent's state — all findings, tool results, and reasoning — against this ID. When the agent resumes after human approval, it picks up exactly where it left off with full context intact.

STAGE B: MULTI-TOOL AGENT WITH CONDITIONAL ROUTING 

Stage B — LangGraph State Machine with Conditional Routing

Stage A uses a single ReAct agent that assesses all dimensions sequentially. Stage B evolves this into a LangGraph state graph where each dimension is a dedicated node, and the agent routes conditionally based on findings. If custom code risk is CRITICAL, the agent flags for immediate escalation rather than continuing to assessment dimensions that won't change the outcome.

START → Custom code node → Route: CRITICAL? → Escalate / Continue → Table + Config + Integration nodes → Score node → Human checkpoint → Report node
Why Conditional Routing Matters for Migration Assessment

A sequential agent always runs all four dimensions regardless of what it finds. A graph-based agent with conditional routing can:

  • Skip downstream checks when a critical blocker makes the overall score irrelevant
  • Run high-risk dimension checks first and escalate early
  • Take different reporting paths based on overall readiness level
  • Pause at different points depending on finding severity

This is the difference between an agent that executes a fixed workflow faster and an agent that genuinely adapts its approach based on what it discovers.

Stage B State Graph Definition

# Stage B: LangGraph state machine with conditional routing
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.checkpoint.memory import MemorySaver
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
import json
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
memory = MemorySaver()
 
# Node: Assess custom code dimension
def assess_custom_code_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_custom_code.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - min(100, result['modification_count'] // 2)
    state['custom_code'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['custom_code'] = score
 
    # Flag critical blocker if modifications exceed threshold
    if result['modification_count'] > 500:
        state['findings'].append({
            'dimension': 'custom_code',
            'severity': 'CRITICAL',
            'message': f"{result['modification_count']} SAP standard modifications detected — clean core remediation required before migration"
        })
        state['human_review_needed'] = True
    return state
 
# Node: Assess table complexity
def assess_table_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_table_complexity.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (20 if result['volume_risk'] == 'HIGH' else
                   10 if result['volume_risk'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['table_complexity'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['table_complexity'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Assess configuration footprint
def assess_config_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_config_footprint.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (30 if result['config_complexity'] == 'HIGH' else
                   15 if result['config_complexity'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['config_footprint'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['config_footprint'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Assess integration dependencies
def assess_integration_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    result = json.loads(assess_integration_dependencies.invoke(
        {'system_id': state['system_id']}
    ))
    score = 100 - (35 if result['integration_risk'] == 'HIGH' else
                   15 if result['integration_risk'] == 'MEDIUM' else 0)
    state['integration_deps'] = result
    state['dimension_scores']['integration_deps'] = score
    return state
 
# Node: Calculate overall score and readiness level
def score_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    scores = state['dimension_scores']
    weighted = (
        scores.get('custom_code', 0)      * 0.30 +
        scores.get('table_complexity', 0) * 0.25 +
        scores.get('config_footprint', 0) * 0.20 +
        scores.get('integration_deps', 0) * 0.25
    )
    state['overall_score'] = round(weighted)
    state['readiness_level'] = (
        'HIGH'     if weighted >= 80 else
        'MEDIUM'   if weighted >= 60 else
        'LOW'      if weighted >= 40 else 'CRITICAL'
    )
    state['human_review_needed'] = True
    return state
 
# Node: Generate final scored report using LLM
def report_node(state: MigrationAssessmentState):
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
    response = llm.invoke([
        SystemMessage(content='''Generate a structured SAP migration
        readiness report. Include: Executive Summary with overall score,
        dimension-level findings with scores, critical blockers,
        prioritized recommendations with effort estimates.
        Be specific and reference actual findings.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from state: {json.dumps({k: v for k, v in state.items() if k != "messages"}, default=str)}')
    ])
    state['final_report'] = response.content
    return state
 
# Conditional routing — escalate immediately if critical blocker found
def route_after_custom_code(state: MigrationAssessmentState) -> str:
    critical = any(f['severity'] == 'CRITICAL' for f in state['findings'])
    return 'escalate' if critical else 'continue_assessment'
 
# Build the state graph
graph = StateGraph(MigrationAssessmentState)
 
# Add nodes
graph.add_node('assess_custom_code', assess_custom_code_node)
graph.add_node('assess_table', assess_table_node)
graph.add_node('assess_config', assess_config_node)
graph.add_node('assess_integration', assess_integration_node)
graph.add_node('score', score_node)
graph.add_node('report', report_node)
 
# Set entry point
graph.set_entry_point('assess_custom_code')
 
# Conditional edge after custom code — escalate or continue
graph.add_conditional_edges(
    'assess_custom_code',
    route_after_custom_code,
    {
        'escalate':            END,
        # CRITICAL: stop and flag for human intervention
        'continue_assessment': 'assess_table'
    }
)
 
# Sequential edges for remaining dimensions
graph.add_edge('assess_table',       'assess_config')
graph.add_edge('assess_config',      'assess_integration')
graph.add_edge('assess_integration', 'score')
 
# Human-in-the-loop: interrupt before report generation
graph.add_edge('score', 'report')
graph.add_edge('report', END)
 
# Compile with checkpointer for human-in-the-loop support
app = graph.compile(
    checkpointer=memory,
    interrupt_before=['report']
    # Pause before report — human reviews score first
)

Running Stage B

# Run Stage B — agent routes conditionally based on findings
initial_state = MigrationAssessmentState(
    goal='Assess S/4HANA migration readiness',
    system_id='PRD',
    custom_code=None, table_complexity=None,
    config_footprint=None, integration_deps=None,
    dimension_scores={}, overall_score=None,
    readiness_level=None, findings=[],
    human_review_needed=False, human_approved=False,
    final_report=None
)
 
config = {'configurable': {'thread_id': 'migration-stageb-001'}}
 
# Phase 1: Agent assesses all dimensions and scores
result = app.invoke(initial_state, config=config)
 
# Check if critical blocker caused early escalation
if result['findings'] and result['final_report'] is None:
    critical = [f for f in result['findings'] if f['severity'] == 'CRITICAL']
    if critical:
        print('CRITICAL BLOCKER DETECTED — escalation required')
        print(json.dumps(critical, indent=2))
else:
    # Human reviews score before report is generated
    print(f"Overall Score: {result['overall_score']}/100"
          f" — Readiness: {result['readiness_level']}")
    print(f"Dimension Scores: {result['dimension_scores']}")
 
    approval = input('Approve final report generation? (yes/no): ')
    if approval.lower() == 'yes':
        result['human_approved'] = True
        final = app.invoke(result, config=config)
        print(final['final_report'])

interrupt_before=['report'] tells LangGraph to pause execution before the report node runs. The agent has completed all assessment nodes and scoring — the state holds all findings and dimension scores — but the final report hasn't been generated yet. This is the correct human-in-the-loop point: the human sees scores and findings, approves, then the report is generated with their oversight recorded in the audit trail.

STAGE A VS STAGE B — WHEN TO USE EACH 

Choosing Between Stage A and Stage B

 

Consideration

Stage A

Stage B

Simplicity

Stage A — single agent, less code, easier to debug

Stage B — explicit graph, more code but clearer execution path

Conditional routing

Stage A — model decides order implicitly

Stage B — routing is explicit and testable

Early escalation

Stage A — agent may complete all steps before flagging critical

Stage B — graph routes to END immediately on CRITICAL finding

Audit trail

Stage A — reasoning trace in message history

Stage B — explicit node execution path plus message history

Recommended for

Stage A — prototyping and smaller landscapes

Stage B — production deployments and complex landscapes

What's Next — Part 3

  • Part 3: SAP Exception Log Triage Agent — the agent monitors logs autonomously, identifies patterns across multiple error types, correlates related issues, and recommends remediation actions without human triage. Introduces the observer pattern for continuous agent monitoring.
  • Part 4: SAP Master Data Quality Agent — detects and prioritizes data quality issues across master data domains, generates a remediation plan with effort scoring, and integrates with the RAG pipeline from Series 1.
  • Part 5: S/4HANA Readiness Agent — extends Part 2 with clean core analysis, deprecated function module detection, and BAdI/enhancement spot mapping.
  • Part 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — all four agents working together under an orchestrator that delegates assessment dimensions and synthesizes findings into a unified landscape report.

The migration assessment agent built in this article is the most complex single-agent implementation in this series. Parts 3–5 follow the same patterns — tool definitions, state schema, conditional routing, human-in-the-loop — applied to different SAP domains. If Stage B makes sense here, the remaining parts will feel familiar.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent (Part 3 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 3 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

From reactive triage to proactive monitoring — a multi-agent system with a router that delegates SM21 system log errors and SM37 job failures to specialized sub-agents, correlates findings across log types, and escalates autonomously.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 3 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 3 introduces the multi-agent pattern — a router agent that classifies incoming SAP errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents: one for SM21 system log analysis, one for SM37 job failure triage. The router also identifies correlations across log types that neither sub-agent sees alone. The article covers two stages: Stage A reactive triage (triggered on demand) and Stage B proactive monitoring (continuous, schedule-driven). This is the architectural foundation for the multi-agent orchestration in Part 6.

Series Context

This is Part 3 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Parts 1 and 2 built single agents — a data quality assessor and a migration assessment agent with conditional routing. Part 3 introduces multi-agent coordination: a router agent that classifies errors and delegates to specialized sub-agents. This pattern is the core building block for Part 6, where all agents in this series work together under a unified orchestrator.

The exception log triage use case is ideal for introducing multi-agent patterns because SAP error landscapes involve genuinely different log types that require different analysis approaches. A single agent handling SM21 and SM37 in the same reasoning chain produces lower quality findings than two specialized agents, each focused on their domain, coordinated by a router that sees the full picture.

WHY MULTI-AGENT FOR SAP LOG TRIAGE 

Why Log Triage Benefits from Multi-Agent Architecture

SAP exception triage in production environments involves multiple log sources that are related but structurally different:

Log Type

What It Contains

Who Cares

SM21 System Log

Platform-level errors — work process failures, memory issues, RFC connection problems, kernel errors. Context is technical infrastructure.

SAP Basis / Platform team

SM37 Job Log

Business process failures — scheduled job errors, batch processing failures, data load problems. Context is business process continuity.

Functional / Operations team

Correlation layer

When an SM37 job fails because of an SM21 RFC connection error — neither log alone tells the full story. The router sees both.

Both teams need joint view

A single agent reasoning over both log types simultaneously tends to conflate the contexts — applying infrastructure reasoning to business process errors and vice versa. Specialized sub-agents with a coordinating router produce more accurate findings and clearer escalation paths.

MULTI-AGENT ARCHITECTURE OVERVIEW 

Router + Sub-Agent Architecture

Error batch input → Router agent classifies → SM21 sub-agent → Correlate findings → Severity score + escalation
		→	SM37 sub-agent	→

The router agent has three responsibilities:

  • Classify incoming errors — determine which log type each error belongs to
  • Delegate to the appropriate specialized sub-agent
  • Correlate findings across both sub-agents — identify cases where SM21 infrastructure errors are causing SM37 job failures

 

Agent

Responsibility

Router agent

Classifies errors, delegates to sub-agents, correlates cross-log findings, produces unified severity score

SM21 sub-agent

Analyzes system log errors — identifies platform issues, RFC failures, memory problems, kernel errors

SM37 sub-agent

Analyzes job log failures — identifies which jobs failed, why, business process impact, retry feasibility

STATE SCHEMA AND TOOLS 

State Schema and Tool Definitions

Triage State Schema
# triage_agent.py — multi-agent exception log triage
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class TriageState(TypedDict):
    raw_errors:         List[dict]   # Incoming error batch
    sm21_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM21 errors
    sm37_errors:        List[dict]   # Classified SM37 errors
    sm21_findings:      List[dict]   # SM21 sub-agent findings
    sm37_findings:      List[dict]   # SM37 sub-agent findings
    correlations:       List[dict]   # Cross-log correlations
    overall_severity:   str          # CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
    escalation_needed:  bool
    escalation_targets: List[str]    # Which teams to notify
    triage_report:      Optional[str]

Tool Definitions

# tools/triage_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def get_sm21_errors(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM21 system log errors from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            MANDT, DATUM, UZEIT, MTYPE,
            MSGID, MSGNO, MSGTX,
            REPID, ABAP_RUNTIME_ERROR
        FROM SYSLOG
        WHERE DATUM >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        AND MTYPE IN ('E', 'A', 'X')
        -- E=Error, A=Abend, X=Exit
        ORDER BY DATUM DESC, UZEIT DESC
        LIMIT 200
    ''')
    errors = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        errors.append({
            'date': str(row[1]), 'time': str(row[2]),
            'type': row[3], 'message_id': row[4],
            'message_text': row[6], 'program': row[7],
            'runtime_error': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm21_errors': errors, 'count': len(errors)})
 
@tool
def get_sm37_failures(hours_back: int = 24) -> str:
    """Fetch recent SM37 job failures from SAP HANA."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            JOBNAME, JOBCOUNT, STATUS,
            SDLSTRTDT, SDLSTRTTM,
            LASTCHGDT, LASTCHGTM,
            JOBCLASS, PRGNAME
        FROM TBTCO
        WHERE STATUS = 'A'
        -- A = Aborted
        AND SDLSTRTDT >= ADD_DAYS(CURRENT_DATE, -{hours_back // 24})
        ORDER BY LASTCHGDT DESC, LASTCHGTM DESC
        LIMIT 100
    ''')
    failures = []
    for row in cursor.fetchall():
        failures.append({
            'job_name': row[0], 'job_count': row[1],
            'status': row[2], 'start_date': str(row[3]),
            'start_time': str(row[4]), 'job_class': row[7],
            'program': row[8]
        })
    return json.dumps({'sm37_failures': failures, 'count': len(failures)})
 
@tool
def get_job_log_details(job_name: str, job_count: str) -> str:
    """Get detailed log messages for a specific failed SM37 job."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT MSGTY, MSGID, MSGNO, MSGV1, MSGV2
        FROM TBTCP
        WHERE JOBNAME = '{job_name}'
        AND JOBCOUNT = '{job_count}'
        AND MSGTY IN ('E', 'A')
        ORDER BY LNSNR
    ''')
    messages = [{'type': r[0], 'msg_id': r[1],
                 'msg_no': r[2], 'text': f'{r[3]} {r[4]}'.strip()}
                for r in cursor.fetchall()]
    return json.dumps({'job': job_name, 'messages': messages})
STAGE A: REACTIVE MULTI-AGENT TRIAGE 

Stage A — Reactive Triage on Demand

Stage A is triggered manually or by an integration call — it receives a batch of errors, classifies them, runs sub-agents, and produces a triage report. This is the right pattern for on-call support workflows and integration with ITSM tools like ServiceNow.

Router Agent

# Router: classifies errors and delegates to sub-agents
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
 
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
router_prompt = '''You are a SAP exception log router agent.
Your job is to:
1. Fetch recent SM21 system log errors using get_sm21_errors
2. Fetch recent SM37 job failures using get_sm37_failures
3. Count and categorize errors by type and severity
4. Identify the top 3 most critical issues requiring immediate attention
5. Note any patterns suggesting SM21 errors are causing SM37 failures
6. Produce a structured classification summary for sub-agent processing

Be systematic. Fetch both log types before drawing conclusions.
Always explain your classification reasoning.'''
 
router_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors, get_sm37_failures],
    state_modifier=router_prompt
)

SM21 Sub-Agent

# SM21 specialist: deep analysis of system log errors
sm21_prompt = '''You are an SAP Basis expert specializing in SM21 system logs.
Analyze the SM21 errors provided and for each significant error:

1. Identify the root cause (infrastructure, memory, RFC, kernel)
2. Assess business impact — which processes are affected
3. Classify severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW
4. Recommend immediate action
5. Recommend preventive measure

Focus on patterns — a single error may be noise,
ten of the same error in one hour is a systemic issue.
Always reference specific error message IDs in your findings.'''
 
sm21_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm21_errors],
    state_modifier=sm21_prompt
)

SM37 Sub-Agent

# SM37 specialist: deep analysis of job failures
sm37_prompt = '''You are an SAP operations expert specializing in SM37 job failures.
For each failed job:

1. Use get_job_log_details to retrieve the specific failure messages
2. Identify the root cause (data issue, RFC failure, timeout, authorization)
3. Assess business process impact — what does this job do?
4. Classify severity based on job class and business criticality
5. Determine if the job can be safely restarted or requires fix-first
6. Identify dependent jobs that may be blocked

Job class A = highest priority. Always investigate class A failures first.'''
 
sm37_agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=[get_sm37_failures, get_job_log_details],
    state_modifier=sm37_prompt
)
Stage A Orchestration — Router + Sub-Agents
# Stage A: Orchestrate router + sub-agents reactively
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
import json
 
def run_reactive_triage(hours_back: int = 24) -> dict:
    """Run full reactive triage across SM21 and SM37 logs."""
 
    # Step 1: Router classifies and summarizes all errors
    router_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Fetch and classify all SAP errors from the last {hours_back} hours.
            Summarize SM21 system errors and SM37 job failures separately.
            Flag any cases where system errors appear to be causing job failures.
        ''')]
    })
    router_summary = router_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 2: SM21 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm21_result = sm21_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM21 system log errors from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm21_findings = sm21_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 3: SM37 sub-agent performs deep analysis
    sm37_result = sm37_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Perform deep analysis of SM37 job failures from
            the last {hours_back} hours. Get job log details for
            class A failures first. Router summary for context:
            {router_summary}
        ''')]
    })
    sm37_findings = sm37_result['messages'][-1].content
 
    # Step 4: Router correlates findings from both sub-agents
    correlation_result = router_agent.invoke({
        'messages': [HumanMessage(content=f'''
            Correlate these findings from both log types and produce
            a unified triage report with:
            1. Overall severity (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW)
            2. Top 3 issues requiring immediate action
            3. Cross-log correlations (SM21 errors causing SM37 failures)
            4. Escalation targets (Basis team / Operations team / Both)
            5. Recommended actions in priority order

            SM21 findings: {sm21_findings}
            SM37 findings: {sm37_findings}
        ''')]
    })
 
    return {
        'router_summary': router_summary,
        'sm21_findings': sm21_findings,
        'sm37_findings': sm37_findings,
        'triage_report': correlation_result['messages'][-1].content
    }
 
# Run reactive triage
result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
print(result['triage_report'])

Passing the router summary as context to each sub-agent is the key pattern here. The sub-agents don't operate in isolation — they receive the router's classification as context, which focuses their analysis on the most significant errors rather than treating every log entry with equal weight.

STAGE B: PROACTIVE MONITORING AGENT 

Stage B — Proactive Monitoring with Scheduled Execution

Stage B wraps the Stage A reactive triage in a monitoring loop. The agent runs on a schedule, compares current error patterns against a baseline, and triggers triage automatically when anomalies are detected — without waiting for a human to initiate the process.

Scheduler triggers → Fetch current errors → Compare to baseline	→ Anomaly detected? → Run reactive triage	→ Escalate if needed

Baseline and Anomaly Detection

# monitoring.py — proactive monitoring wrapper
import json, time, os
from datetime import datetime
 
def get_error_baseline() -> dict:
    """Load stored baseline error counts for comparison."""
    # In production: store baseline in HANA or BTP configuration
    return {
        'sm21_hourly_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM21_BASELINE', '10')),
        'sm37_daily_avg': int(os.environ.get('SM37_BASELINE', '5')),
        'anomaly_threshold': 2.0
        # Trigger if errors > 2x baseline
    }
 
def check_for_anomalies(current_counts: dict, baseline: dict) -> dict:
    """Compare current error counts against baseline."""
    sm21_ratio = current_counts.get('sm21_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm21_hourly_avg'], 1)
    sm37_ratio = current_counts.get('sm37_count', 0) / max(baseline['sm37_daily_avg'], 1)
    threshold = baseline['anomaly_threshold']
 
    return {
        'anomaly_detected': sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold,
        'sm21_ratio': round(sm21_ratio, 2),
        'sm37_ratio': round(sm37_ratio, 2),
        'trigger_reason': (
            f'SM21 errors at {sm21_ratio:.1f}x baseline' if sm21_ratio > threshold
            else f'SM37 failures at {sm37_ratio:.1f}x baseline'
        ) if sm21_ratio > threshold or sm37_ratio > threshold else None
    }
 
def send_escalation(triage_report: str, targets: list):
    """Send escalation via Microsoft Graph API or Teams webhook."""
    # Integration with MS Graph covered in the training automation article
    # For BTP CF deployment: use requests to call Graph API
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Escalation sent to: {targets}')
    print(triage_report[:500])
    # Truncated for logging
 
def run_monitoring_cycle():
    """Single monitoring cycle — run on schedule via CF Scheduler."""
    print(f'[{datetime.now()}] Starting monitoring cycle...')
 
    # Quick error count check — lightweight, runs every cycle
    sm21_data = json.loads(get_sm21_errors.invoke({'hours_back': 1}))
    sm37_data = json.loads(get_sm37_failures.invoke({'hours_back': 24}))
 
    current_counts = {
        'sm21_count': sm21_data.get('count', 0),
        'sm37_count': sm37_data.get('count', 0)
    }
 
    baseline = get_error_baseline()
    anomaly = check_for_anomalies(current_counts, baseline)
 
    if anomaly['anomaly_detected']:
        print(f'Anomaly detected: {anomaly["trigger_reason"]}')
        # Run full reactive triage
        result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=4)
        # Triage last 4 hours on anomaly
 
        # Escalate if triage confirms high severity
        if 'CRITICAL' in result['triage_report'] or 'HIGH' in result['triage_report']:
            send_escalation(
                result['triage_report'],
                targets=['basis-team@company.com', 'operations@company.com']
            )
    else:
        print(f'No anomaly. SM21: {current_counts["sm21_count"]} errors,
              SM37: {current_counts["sm37_count"]} failures — within baseline.')
 
# BTP CF Flask entry point — triggered by CF Scheduler
from flask import Flask, jsonify
app = Flask(__name__)
 
@app.route('/monitor', methods=['POST'])
def monitor():
    run_monitoring_cycle()
    return jsonify({'status': 'ok', 'timestamp': datetime.now().isoformat()})
 
@app.route('/triage', methods=['POST'])
def triage():
    # Manual reactive triage endpoint
    result = run_reactive_triage(hours_back=24)
    return jsonify(result)

The baseline values (SM21_BASELINE, SM37_BASELINE) are set as BTP environment variables in your manifest.yml. Start with conservative baselines — set them to your current average error counts — and tighten them over time as you observe the agent's sensitivity. Anomaly thresholds that are too low produce alert fatigue. Start at 2x baseline and adjust based on false positive rate.

STAGE A VS STAGE B 

When to Use Each Pattern

Consideration

Stage A — Reactive

Stage B — Proactive

Trigger

Manual or integration call

Scheduled — runs every N minutes

Use case

On-call support, ITSM integration, ad-hoc investigation

Production monitoring, overnight batch, continuous operations

Cost

Runs only when called — lower LLM cost

Runs on schedule — baseline check is lightweight, full triage only on anomaly

Deployment

REST endpoint on BTP CF

CF Scheduler + REST endpoint on BTP CF

Recommended for

Support teams, incident response workflows

Operations teams, 24x7 production environments

NEW PATTERN: ROUTER + SUB-AGENT COORDINATION
